Develops: Critical ethical reasoning, empathy, professional identity
Example: Case of informed consent refusal; breaking bad news
2. Role Play
Students enact doctor-patient scenarios in pairs/small groups
Immediate feedback by peers and facilitator
Develops: IPC skills, empathy, conflict resolution
3. Standardized Patients (SP) / Simulated Clinical Encounters
Trained actors portray patients with specific communication challenges
Provides safe environment to practice without harming real patients
Assessed via OSCE stations
4. Reflective Writing / Journaling
Students write reflective accounts after clinical postings
Guided questions: 'What did I feel? What would I do differently?'
Develops: Self-awareness, professional identity, emotional intelligence
5. Small Group Discussion (SGD)
Facilitated structured discussions on ethical dilemmas, professionalism
Promotes peer learning, democratic dialogue
6. Lecture-Demonstration
Didactic for foundational ethics principles (beneficence, non-maleficence, autonomy, justice)
Demonstration of communication techniques by faculty with feedback
7. Portfolio-Based Learning
Students compile evidence of their communication and professional growth
Includes reflective logs, peer assessments, supervisor feedback
8. Bedside Teaching with Communication Focus
Faculty model effective doctor-patient communication at bedside
Students observe, then practice under supervision
'See one, do one, teach one' modified for communication skills
9. Arts and Humanities (Narrative Medicine)
Literature, films, artwork used to explore illness experience
Develops empathy, reduces burnout, enhances humanistic medicine
Assessment of AETCOM Competencies
Formative: OSCE, mini-CEX, peer assessment, reflective portfolios
Summative: Communication OSCE stations, professionalism portfolio
360-degree feedback: Patient, peer, supervisor

37

subsections.title: 3. INDICATORS OF HEALTH
subsections.content:
(MORBIDITY / MORTALITY /

DISABILITY / HEALTH SYSTEMS / FERTILITY)

Q1. Morbidity and mortality indicators of health — describe in detail with examples. (2022,

2018) ★★★★★

Introduction — Health Indicators
Health indicators are quantitative measures that describe the health status of a population and allow comparisons
across time, place, and sub-groups. They are essential for health planning, monitoring, and evaluation (WHO
Health Statistics).

A. MORBIDITY INDICATORS

Morbidity = burden of illness, disease, and injury in a population.
1. Incidence Rate
Number of NEW cases of a disease per 1000 population per year
Formula: (New cases / Population at risk) × 1000
Use: Epidemic investigation, vaccine efficacy studies
Example: TB incidence in India = 199/1,00,000 (2022, WHO GTB)
2. Prevalence Rate
Number of ALL cases (old + new) at a given time per 1000 population
Point prevalence: At a specific point in time
Period prevalence: During a defined period
Example: Hypertension prevalence India ~28% adults (ICMR-INDIAB)
3. Attack Rate
Incidence proportion during limited epidemic period
Formula: (Cases during epidemic / Exposed population) × 100
Use: Outbreak investigation (food poisoning, cholera)
4. Secondary Attack Rate (SAR)
Proportion of susceptible household contacts who develop disease after index case
Measures transmissibility within households
5. Disability Rates
Days of disability per person per year
DALY = Disability Adjusted Life Year (YLL + YLD)
Example: India's disease burden — 29% from NCDs expressed in DALYs

B. MORTALITY INDICATORS

Indicator
Formula
India Value
Crude Death Rate (CDR)
Deaths / mid-yr pop × 1000
6.2/1000 (SRS 2020)
Infant Mortality Rate (IMR)
Deaths <1yr / LB × 1000

28/1000 LB (SRS 2020)

Under-5 Mortality Rate (U5MR)
Deaths <5yr / LB × 1000

32/1000 LB

Neonatal Mortality Rate (NMR)
Deaths <28d / LB × 1000

20/1000 LB

Maternal Mortality Ratio (MMR)
Maternal deaths / 1,00,000 LB

97/1,00,000 (SRS 2018-20)

Case Fatality Rate (CFR)
Deaths / Cases × 100
COVID CFR India ~1.2%
38

Indicator
Formula
India Value
Proportional Mortality Rate
Deaths from X / Total deaths ×
100
CVD 28% of all deaths
Life Expectancy at Birth
Model life table calculation
India 70.19 yrs (2021)

Sensitive Indicators of Health
IMR — most sensitive single indicator of overall health status (reflects MCH care quality)
MMR — reflects obstetric care, antenatal services, emergency response
U5MR — MDG/SDG target indicator for child health

Q2. Discuss various indicators of health to assess health profile of a community. (2015, 2011)

Classification of Health Indicators (WHO / Park)
A. Mortality Indicators
CDR, Age-specific death rates, IMR, NMR, U5MR, MMR, PMR, CFR
Life expectancy at birth and at specific ages
PYLL (Potential Years of Life Lost)
B. Morbidity Indicators
Incidence and prevalence rates of specific diseases
Attack rates, SAR, notification rates
Hospital admission/readmission rates
C. Disability Indicators
DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Year) = YLL + YLD
QALY (Quality-Adjusted Life Year)
Days of restricted activity / days of bed disability per person/year
WHO Disability Assessment Schedule (WHODAS)
D. Nutritional Status Indicators
Anthropometric: Weight-for-age (stunting), height-for-age (wasting), MUAC
Biochemical: Hb level (anaemia), serum vitamin A, urinary iodine
India: NFHS-5: 35.5% stunted, 19.3% wasted, 32.1% underweight <5 yrs
E. Health Care Delivery Indicators
Doctor-population ratio (India: 1:834 — above WHO norm of 1:1000)
Nurse-population ratio, bed-population ratio
Population per health sub-centre, PHC, CHC
Immunization coverage (DPT3, measles)
F. Health Policy Indicators
% GDP on health expenditure (India: 2.1% 2021-22, target 2.5% by 2025)
% health budget for primary health care
Access to essential medicines
G. Socioeconomic Indicators
Literacy rate (India 77.7% — NFHS-5)
Per capita income, Gini coefficient (inequality)
% population below poverty line
39

Safe water access, sanitation coverage (ODF — Swachh Bharat)
H. Social and Mental Health Indicators
Suicide rate, alcohol/substance abuse prevalence
Crime rate, domestic violence incidence
Composite Indices
Index
Components
Use
Human Development Index

(HDI)

Life expectancy + Education + GNI per capita

UNDP

Physical Quality of Life
Index (PQLI)
IMR + Life expectancy at age 1 + Literacy
Morris
Human Poverty Index (HPI)
Survival, literacy, living standard

UNDP

Gender Development Index
HDI disaggregated by sex

UNDP

Q3. Elaborate on Disability rates used as indicators of health. (2022, 2018, 2014) ★★★★★
Definition
Disability refers to limitations in functioning resulting from health conditions — it encompasses impairments,
activity limitations, and participation restrictions (ICF 2001, WHO).
Types of Disability Measures
1. Days of Disability (National Health Survey method)
Restricted activity days: Days when person cannot perform usual activities
Bed disability days: Days spent in bed >50% of the day due to illness
Work-loss days: Days absent from work due to illness
Average: 2–4 restricted activity days per person per year (USA-NCHS)
2. Disability-Adjusted Life Year (DALY)
DALY = Years of Life Lost (YLL) + Years Lived with Disability (YLD)
YLL = N (deaths) × L (standard life expectancy at age of death)
YLD = Number of cases × Disability weight × Duration
One DALY = one year of healthy life lost
Global Burden of Disease Study uses DALY as primary summary measure
India: TB causes 2.7 million DALYs; lower respiratory infections — 2.5M DALYs (GBD 2019)
3. Quality-Adjusted Life Year (QALY)
Used in health economics and technology assessment
QALY = Years lived × Quality weight (0–1 scale, 1 = perfect health)
Used to calculate Cost per QALY gained — threshold ~$50,000/QALY (WHO-CHOICE)
4. WHO Disability Assessment Schedule (WHODAS 2.0)
36-item interview-administered scale
Domains: Cognition, Mobility, Self-care, Getting along, Life activities, Participation
Provides disability score 0–100 (0 = no disability, 100 = maximum disability)
5. Sullivan's Index (Disability-Free Life Expectancy, DFLE)
Combines life expectancy and disability prevalence
DFLE = Years expected to be lived WITHOUT disability
More sensitive measure than life expectancy alone
40

Census/Legal Disability Classification in India
Type
Criteria

18.8% of disabled

18.9%

7.5%

Q4. Health systems indicators. (Oct 2025) ★NEW★
Definition
Health system indicators measure the inputs, processes, and outputs of a health system, enabling assessment of
system performance, equity, and effectiveness.
Framework: WHO Health System Building Blocks
Building Block
Key Indicators
Service delivery
OPD visits/person/year, hospital bed occupancy rate, ANC coverage,
immunization coverage
Health workforce
Doctor:population ratio (India 1:834), nurse:pop ratio, ASHA workers
deployed (>1M)
Health information
HMIS data completeness, vital registration coverage, disease notification
rates
Medical products
Essential medicines availability, vaccine cold chain coverage, stock-out
frequency
Health financing
% GDP on health (India 2.1%), out-of-pocket expenditure (51%), per
capita health spending
Leadership/governance
Health policy implementation score, intersectoral coordination, National
Health Mission progress

Key Facility-Level Health System Indicators:
Doctor-population ratio: WHO standard 1:1000; India 1:834 (NHM 2022)
Nurse-midwife to population: WHO recommended 3/1000; India 2.1/1000
Beds per 1000 population: WHO norm 3.5; India 0.5 (public) — overall 1.4
PHC coverage: 1/30,000 population (plain areas), 1/20,000 (tribal/hilly)
Immunization coverage: DPT3 93%, MCV1 93% (NFHS-5, 2021)
Antenatal care: 4+ ANC visits — 58.1% (NFHS-5)
Institutional delivery rate: 88.6% (NFHS-5)

Q5. Describe the measurement of health status of the community through various health
indicators. (Dec 2024) ★NEW★
Framework
41

Measuring community health status requires a multi-dimensional approach using positive, negative, and
composite indicators across biological, social, and environmental domains.
Approach: Positive + Negative Health Indicators
Dimension
Positive Indicators
Negative Indicators
Biological
Life expectancy, DFLE, nutritional status

IMR, MMR, CDR, DALY

Social
Literacy, HDI, income, social support
Poverty rate, crime,
unemployment
Environmental
Safe water %, sanitation %, AQI
Pollution levels, vector density
Healthcare
Doctor ratio, bed ratio, coverage
Hospital-acquired infections

Measurement Methods
Population census: Demographic data, literacy, household structure
Sample Registration System (SRS): IMR, CDR, TFR, MMR in India
National Family Health Survey (NFHS): Comprehensive MCH, nutrition indicators
HMIS/DHIS2: Routine service delivery data
Disease notification systems: Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme (IDSP)
Disease registries: National Cancer Registry Programme, CBHI hospital data

Q6. Health care delivery indicators. (May 2024, May 2025) ★★★★
Definition
Healthcare delivery indicators measure the availability, accessibility, utilization, and quality of health services
provided to a population.
Classification
A. Availability Indicators:
Doctor:population ratio — India 1:834 (NMC 2022; WHO norm 1:1000)
Beds per 1000 population — India 1.4 overall, public 0.5
Facilities per population — PHC 1/30,000; CHC 1/1,20,000
B. Accessibility Indicators:
% population within 5 km of health facility
% households with health insurance (Ayushman Bharat — >50 crore beneficiaries)
Out-of-pocket expenditure as % of total health expenditure (India 51%)
C. Utilization Indicators:
OPD visits per person per year
Bed occupancy rate (ideal 80-85%)
Average length of stay (ALOS)
Antenatal care coverage (4+ visits), institutional delivery rate
Immunization coverage (DPT3, measles)
D. Quality Indicators:
Maternal near-miss rate, complication rates
C-section rate (WHO recommended 10–15%; India national 21.5% NFHS-5)
Readmission rates, surgical site infection rates
Patient satisfaction scores
42

Q7. List the Morbidity Indicators. Add a note on International Classification of Diseases. (2015)

Morbidity Indicators — Summary
Incidence rate: New cases/at-risk population × 1000
Prevalence rate (point and period)
Attack rate and Secondary attack rate
Hospitalization rate: Admissions/1000 population/year
Sickness absence rate: Days lost/employed person/year
Disability rates: DALY, QALY, restricted activity days
Notification rate: Reported cases of notifiable diseases
International Classification of Diseases (ICD)
Background:
ICD is the WHO standard for coding diseases, symptoms, and causes of death
Developed in 1893 (Bertillon Classification), revised 11 times
Current edition: ICD-11 (adopted by WHO in 2019, effective Jan 2022)
India uses ICD-10 in most facilities; transitioning to ICD-11
Structure of ICD-11:
22 chapters covering all diseases, injuries, external causes, and Z-codes (factors influencing health)
Digital-first design: web-based, multilingual, linkable to clinical terminologies (SNOMED CT)
Separate chapters for Traditional Medicine (Chapter 26) — reflects Ayurveda, TCM
ICD-11 codes: e.g., TB pulmonary = 1B10, HIV = 1C62
Uses of ICD:
Morbidity coding in hospitals — DRG-based payment, quality benchmarking
Mortality statistics — cause of death certification
Epidemiological research — disease burden studies
Health system management — resource allocation, planning
International comparisons of disease burden

Q8. Explain quality of life and its calculation. (2020, 2015) ★★★
Definition
Quality of Life (QoL): 'An individual's perception of their position in life in the context of the culture and value
systems in which they live and in relation to their goals, expectations, standards, and concerns.' — WHO, 1997
Health-Related Quality of Life (HRQoL) focuses on the impact of health and disease on physical, psychological,
social, and functional well-being.
Domains of QoL (WHOQOL Framework)
Physical: Pain, energy, sleep, mobility, ADL
Psychological: Emotions, cognition, body image, self-esteem
Social relationships: Personal relationships, social support, sexual activity
Environmental: Safety, finances, transport, leisure
Level of independence: Mobility, communication, work capacity
Spiritual/religion: Personal beliefs, meaning of life
43

Instruments for Measurement
Instrument
Type
Items
Use

WHOQOL-BREF

Generic
26
General population, cross-cultural
research

SF-36

Generic
36
Clinical trials, chronic disease

EQ-5D

Generic
5
Health economics, QALY
calculation

FACT-G

Disease-specific
27
Cancer patients
KAPS score
Disease-specific
Varies
Specific chronic conditions

QALY Calculation
QALY = Years of life × Utility weight (health state value 0–1)
Example: Patient lives 10 years with moderate angina (utility 0.7):
QALY = 10 × 0.7 = 7 QALYs
Used in health technology assessment — NICE UK threshold: £20,000–30,000/QALY

Q9. Enumerate IMR causes. Why is IMR considered a sensitive indicator of health? (2017) ★★
Definition
Infant Mortality Rate (IMR) = (Deaths in infants <1 year / Live births in same year) × 1000
India IMR: 28/1000 live births (SRS 2020) — down from 80 in 1990. Target: <20 by 2025 (NHM).
Causes of Infant Mortality
Neonatal deaths (0–28 days) — 67% of IMR:
Preterm birth complications (35%)
Birth asphyxia and trauma (25%)
Neonatal sepsis/infections (15%)
Congenital anomalies (5%)
Post-neonatal deaths (29 days–<1 year) — 33% of IMR:
Pneumonia/ALRI (leading cause)
Diarrhoeal diseases
Malnutrition (underlying cause in 45% of child deaths)
Malaria, measles in endemic areas
Why IMR is a Sensitive Health Indicator
13. Reflects overall health system quality: Captures MCH services, immunization, nutrition, sanitation
simultaneously
14. Sensitive to socioeconomic conditions: Poverty, literacy, maternal education all directly impact IMR
15. Rapidly responsive to interventions: Improvements in ANC, skilled delivery, immunization reflect quickly in
IMR
16. International comparability: Standardized definition enables cross-national comparison
17. Equity marker: Wide urban-rural, state-wise variations in IMR reveal health inequities (Bihar 46 vs Kerala

6 — SRS 2020)

18. Policy target: Central to NHM, SDG 3.2 (U5MR <25/1000 LB by 2030)

44

Q10. Status of fertility indicators in India. (Oct 2025) ★NEW★
Key Fertility Indicators
Indicator
Definition
India Value (NFHS-5)
Crude Birth Rate (CBR)
Live births/1000 mid-yr pop
19.5/1000 (SRS 2020)
General Fertility Rate (GFR)
LB/1000 women 15-44 yrs
Declining
Total Fertility Rate (TFR)
Avg children per woman lifetime
2.0 (NFHS-5, 2021)
Net Reproduction Rate (NRR)
Daughters surviving to
reproductive age
~0.95 (replacement ~1.0)
Age-specific Fertility Rate
LB/1000 women in specific age
group
Peak: 20-24 yrs
Adolescent Fertility Rate
Births/1000 women 15-19 yrs
6.8% (NFHS-5)

Key Highlights — India Fertility Status
TFR reached replacement level (2.1) for first time in NFHS-5 (2019-21) — landmark
State variation: TFR Bihar 2.98 vs Tamil Nadu 1.8 — widest gap
Unmet need for family planning: 9.4% (NFHS-5) — still significant
Contraceptive Prevalence Rate (CPR): 66.7% (NFHS-5)
Female sterilization: 37.9% — overly reliant on terminal method
Modern reversible methods (IUD, pills, condom): 15.7% — need expansion
National Population Policy Targets
TFR 2.1 (replacement level) — ACHIEVED at national level (NFHS-5)
Infant Mortality Rate <30/1000 LB — achieved (28, SRS 2020)
MMR <100/1,00,000 LB by 2020 — partially achieved (97, SRS 2018-20)
Universal access to contraception — ongoing

45

subsections.title: 4. LEVELS OF PREVENTION / MODES OF INTERVENTION / NATURAL HISTORY OF DISEASE
subsections.content:
LEVELS OF PREVENTION / MODES OF INTERVENTION /

NATURAL HISTORY OF DISEASE

Q1. Describe various levels of prevention and modes of intervention with examples. (2018,

2015, 2014) ★★★★★

Introduction
Prevention is the cornerstone of public health. Clark and Leavell (1965) described levels of prevention based on
the natural history of disease and the host-agent-environment triad.
Levels of Prevention
Level
Stage of Disease
Mode of Intervention
Example
Primordial
Pre-disease
(population level)
Societal/environmental
change
Tobacco control laws,
junk food taxes
Primary
Susceptibility stage
Health promotion + Specific
protection
Immunization, health
education, safe water
Secondary
Presymptomatic +
early disease
Early diagnosis + Prompt
treatment
Cancer screening, VIA,
ICTC, neonatal
screening
Tertiary
Advanced disease +
Disability
Disability limitation +
Rehabilitation
Dialysis, prosthetics,
AYUSH for palliative
care

Detailed Description
1. PRIMORDIAL PREVENTION
Concept: Prevent emergence of risk factors before disease begins (Strasser, 1978)
Target: Entire population (healthy individuals), societal determinants
Modes: Policy, legislation, urban planning, taxation
Examples in India:
◦
COTPA: Cigarette & Other Tobacco Products Act — restricts tobacco advertising
◦
Swachh Bharat Mission: Sanitation to prevent diarrhoeal disease risk factors
◦
Junk food taxation to prevent obesity
◦
Building cycle lanes to promote physical activity
2. PRIMARY PREVENTION
A. Health Promotion:
◦
Health education, nutrition promotion, maternal and child care
◦
Adequate housing, recreation, genetic counselling
B. Specific Protection:
◦
Immunization (UIP: BCG, OPV, DPT, Measles, HPV)
◦
Use of specific nutrients: Iodized salt (IDD), iron supplementation (anaemia)
◦
Environmental sanitation: Safe water, food safety, vector control
◦
Protection from occupational hazards: Mask, gloves, safety equipment
◦
Chemoprophylaxis: Malaria — primaquine; TB — INH prophylaxis
3. SECONDARY PREVENTION
A. Early Diagnosis and Prompt Treatment:
46

◦
Objective: Halt progression, reduce severity, prevent complications
◦
Methods: Screening (population/individual), case-finding
◦
Screening tests: VIA for cervical cancer, mammography for breast cancer, CBE
◦
Neonatal screening: TSH for congenital hypothyroidism, PKU
◦
TB: DSTB — 2HRZE/4HR; drug sensitivity testing by CB-NAAT
B. Disability Limitation:
◦
Treat to prevent complications: DM — glucose control to prevent nephropathy
◦
Adequate treatment to prevent disability — leprosy MDT to prevent nerve damage
4. TERTIARY PREVENTION
A. Disability Limitation (advanced)
B. Rehabilitation: Restore maximum possible function
Medical rehabilitation: Dialysis, prosthetics, surgery
Psychological: Counselling, support groups
Social: Vocational training, employment support
Vocational rehabilitation: NIMH, National Leprosy Eradication Programme (NLEP) support
Example: Physiotherapy + vocational training for post-stroke disability
Modes of Intervention — Additional
Besides Leavell & Clark's levels, WHO adds:
Health promotion (Ottawa Charter 1986): Build healthy public policy, create supportive environments,
develop personal skills, strengthen community action, reorient health services
Specific protection: Immunization, chemoprophylaxis, chemical protection
Health education: KAP modification at all levels

Q2. Define natural history of disease. Apply levels of prevention to natural history with
examples. (2023) ★★★★★★
Natural History of Disease
Natural history of disease (NHD) refers to the progression of a disease process in an individual over time, in the
absence of any therapeutic intervention. It encompasses the entire spectrum from initial exposure to an etiological
agent to recovery, disability, or death.
Stages of Natural History of Disease (Leavell & Clark Model)
STAGE 1: Pre-pathogenesis Period (Before disease onset)
Interaction between host, agent, and environment occurs
No clinical signs; disease not yet established in host
Stimulus = etiological agent; host susceptibility determines outcome
Example: Person exposed to M. tuberculosis from a smear-positive contact
STAGE 2: Pathogenesis Period (Disease established in host)
a) Early pathogenesis (Subclinical/Presymptomatic):
◦
Agent multiplies/spreads; pathological changes begin
◦
No symptoms yet; pathological lesion present
◦
Example: Primary TB complex developing in lung without symptoms
b) Discernible early lesion:
◦
Earliest detectable clinical/lab signs
◦
Horizon of symptoms = point when symptoms first appear
c) Advanced disease:
47

◦
Full clinical disease with symptoms, signs, complications
d) Outcome:
◦
Recovery (complete/partial), Disability, Death, or Carrier state
Application of Levels of Prevention — Tuberculosis (TB) Model
Stage of NHD
Level of Prevention
Intervention
Pre-pathogenesis (pre-
exposure)
Primordial
Nutrition, poverty reduction, housing
improvement
Pre-pathogenesis
(exposure stage)
Primary
BCG vaccination, INH prophylaxis for contacts,
health education on cough hygiene
Early pathogenesis
(subclinical)
Secondary
Sputum screening of contacts, CBNAAT (Xpert
MTB/RIF), mass miniature radiography
Clinical disease
Secondary
Prompt initiation of DOTS/DRTB regimen under

NTEP

Advanced/Disability
Tertiary
Nutritional support (Nikshay Poshan Yojana),
disability assessment, vocational rehabilitation

Application to Type 2 Diabetes — NHD Model
Pre-pathogenesis: Sedentary lifestyle, obesity, family history → Primordial (activity promotion, junk food
tax) + Primary (lifestyle counselling, weight loss)
Subclinical (impaired fasting glucose): Secondary — OGTT screening, lifestyle intervention
Clinical DM: Secondary — metformin, insulin, HbA1c monitoring
Complications (retinopathy, nephropathy): Tertiary — laser therapy, dialysis, foot care

Q3. How do you relate levels of prevention to natural history of disease? (Nov 2023) ★★★★★
Conceptual Bridge
The natural history of disease and levels of prevention are inextricably linked in the Leavell & Clark (1965) model.
Each stage of natural history corresponds to a specific level of prevention and mode of intervention.
Key Concept — The Horizon of Symptoms:
Separates the 'iceberg' of subclinical disease from clinically apparent disease
Secondary prevention targets the subclinical phase (before the horizon) — most impactful
The 'iceberg phenomenon' shows most disease is subclinical — screening is essential
Diagrammatic Conceptual Summary
Phase
NHD Stage
Prevention Level
Key Actions
Pre-disease
Ecological phase: host-
agent-env interaction
Primordial + Primary
Modify environment, protect host
Sub-clinical
Stimulus → early lesion
(below horizon)
Secondary (early
diagnosis)
Screen and detect early
Clinical
Discernible lesion →
advanced disease
Secondary (prompt
treatment)
Treat, prevent complications
Post-disease
Disability / Convalescence
Tertiary
Rehabilitate, restore function

48

Q4. Describe the modes of intervention in the natural history of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. (Dec

2024) ★NEW★

Natural History of T2DM
T2DM has a prolonged natural history spanning 10–20 years from initial insulin resistance to full clinical disease
and complications, offering multiple opportunities for preventive intervention.
Stage 1: Pre-pathogenesis — Risk Factor Phase
Risk factors: Obesity (especially central), sedentary lifestyle, family history, ethnicity (South Asians at
higher risk at lower BMI)
Host factors: Genetic predisposition, age, sex
Environmental: Dietary patterns, urbanization, stress
Primordial prevention: Policy — junk food taxation, urban planning for walkability, school nutrition
programmes
Primary prevention: Individual — lifestyle modification (500 kcal/day deficit, 150 min/week moderate
exercise = DPP trial: 58% risk reduction)
Stage 2: Subclinical — Impaired Glucose Metabolism
Impaired Fasting Glucose (IFG): FPG 100–125 mg/dL
Impaired Glucose Tolerance (IGT): 2-hr OGTT 140–199 mg/dL
Prediabetes phase — no symptoms but metabolic derangement present
Secondary prevention (early diagnosis): NHM HWC screening protocol (opportunistic screening >30 yrs
with risk factors)
Metformin for high-risk prediabetes (ADA recommendation)
Stage 3: Clinical T2DM
Symptoms: Polydipsia, polyuria, polyphagia, fatigue, blurred vision
Diagnosis: FPG >126 mg/dL OR 2-hr OGTT >200 mg/dL OR HbA1c >6.5%
Secondary prevention (prompt treatment): MNT + oral hypoglycaemics + lifestyle
SMBG (self-monitoring blood glucose), HbA1c target <7%
Stage 4: Complications — Disability Limitation
Complication
Intervention
Level
Diabetic retinopathy
Annual ophthalmological review, laser
photocoagulation
Tertiary
Diabetic nephropathy
ACEI/ARB, dialysis, transplant
Tertiary
Diabetic neuropathy
Gabapentin, foot care clinics
Tertiary
Diabetic foot
Wound care, podiatry, amputation prevention
Tertiary
CVD
Statins, anti-hypertensives, cardiac rehab
Tertiary

Q5. Discuss the role of primordial prevention in control of Non-communicable diseases. (2013)

Concept of Primordial Prevention
Primordial prevention (Strasser, 1978; WHO 1998) aims to prevent the emergence and establishment of social,
economic, and cultural patterns of living that are known to increase the risk of disease. It acts BEFORE risk
factors develop — preventing the conditions that give rise to risk factors.
Rationale for Primordial Prevention in NCDs
NCDs (CVD, diabetes, COPD, cancers) now account for 63% of global deaths (WHO 2023)
49

India: NCDs responsible for 66% of deaths — premature NCD deaths 4.5 million/year
Common NCD risk factors: Tobacco, physical inactivity, unhealthy diet, harmful alcohol use
Primordial prevention targets the upstream determinants — more cost-effective than treatment
Modes of Primordial Prevention for NCDs
1. Policy and Legislative Interventions:
COTPA (Cigarettes & Tobacco Products Act, 2003): Ban on tobacco advertising, smoking in public places
FSSAI regulations: Front-of-pack nutrition labelling, trans fat elimination, junk food standards
Motor vehicle safety laws: Seat belt, helmet mandates — prevent trauma
Alcohol policy: Liquor licensing restrictions, minimum legal drinking age
Building codes: Mandatory staircase access to reduce sedentary behaviour
2. Environmental and Urban Planning:
Cycle lanes, pedestrian footpaths — Active transport infrastructure
Urban green spaces — Parks, exercise areas
Air quality management — NCAP (National Clean Air Programme) to reduce COPD risk
Safe water and sanitation — prevent infection that triggers NCD exacerbations
3. Fiscal Measures:
Sugar-sweetened beverage tax (India GST on aerated drinks 28%)
Tobacco excise duties — price elasticity of demand reduces consumption
Subsidies on fruits, vegetables, whole grains
4. Health Promotion and Education (Population Level):
School-based programmes: Healthy diet, physical activity habits before risk factors emerge
Media campaigns: Fit India Movement, Eat Right India, Poshan Maah
Community-level social norms change — denormalize tobacco, alcohol
5. Intersectoral Action (HiAP — Health in All Policies):
Agriculture: Support production of healthier foods
Education: Integrate health literacy into school curriculum
Transport: Promote public transport, cycling infrastructure
Workplace: No smoking zones, healthy canteens, mental health support
India-Specific Primordial Prevention Initiatives for NCDs
Initiative
NCD Targeted
Mechanism
National Tobacco Control
Programme (NTCP)
Cancer, COPD, CVD
COTPA enforcement, cessation services
Eat Right India (FSSAI)
Obesity, Diabetes
Healthy food environment, labelling
Fit India Movement
Obesity, CVD,
Diabetes
Physical activity promotion
National Clean Air Programme
COPD, Lung Cancer
Reduce PM2.5 pollution
Swachh Bharat Abhiyan
Infection-linked NCDs
ODF status, hygiene
School Health Programme

(RBSK)

Obesity, dental,
vision
Early screening and habits

Q6. Write in detail about the Pre-pathogenic period with examples. (2015) ★★★★
Pre-Pathogenic Period — Definition
50

The pre-pathogenic period refers to the phase of natural history of disease that occurs BEFORE the disease
process begins in the host. During this stage, the interplay between the host, agent, and environment
(epidemiological triad) creates conditions favorable for disease development, but the pathological process has
NOT yet been established.
Ecological Interactions in Pre-pathogenesis
Epidemiological Triad: HOST ↔ AGENT ↔ ENVIRONMENT
1. Host Factors:
Genetic: Sickle cell trait, BRCA1/2 mutation, HLA-B27
Age and sex: IMR higher in males; breast cancer higher in females
Nutritional status: Malnutrition → susceptibility to TB, measles
Immune status: Immunocompromised → opportunistic infections
Behaviour: Smoking, alcohol, promiscuity
Pre-existing disease: DM increases TB risk 3-fold
2. Agent Factors:
Biological: Infectivity, pathogenicity, virulence, antigenicity
Chemical: Dose, route of entry, metabolic activation
Physical: Duration of exposure, intensity (radiation, noise)
Nutritional: Deficiency (vitamin A → xerophthalmia) or excess (fat → obesity)
3. Environmental Factors:
Physical: Climate, season (malaria peaks in monsoon), geography
Biological: Vector density (Aedes mosquito), reservoir hosts
Social: Overcrowding (TB), poverty, education
Economic: Poverty → malnutrition → increased susceptibility
Examples of Pre-pathogenic Period
Example 1 — Tuberculosis:
Host: Malnutritioned adolescent, HIV co-infection
Agent: M. tuberculosis — high infectivity, moderate virulence
Environment: Overcrowded urban slum, poor ventilation
Pre-pathogenesis: Person breathes in droplet nuclei from smear-positive patient
Stage: Ecological phase — agent not yet established in host tissues
Prevention: BCG, ventilation, nutritional support (all PRIMARY prevention)
Example 2 — Coronary Heart Disease (CHD):
Host: Middle-aged male, sedentary, family history of DM
Agent: Risk factors — hypercholesterolaemia, hypertension, diabetes
Environment: Urban lifestyle, junk food availability, work stress
Pre-pathogenesis: Risk factors accumulating silently over years before first MI
Prevention: Lifestyle modification, statin, BP control (PRIMARY + PRIMORDIAL)
Example 3 — Cervical Cancer:
Host: Young woman, multiple sexual partners, low socioeconomic status
Agent: HPV type 16/18 — carcinogenic
Environment: Poor hygiene, lack of screening access
Pre-pathogenesis: HPV exposure before any cellular change
Prevention: HPV vaccine 9–14 yrs, safe sex education
Significance of Pre-pathogenic Period for Public Health
The MOST cost-effective time to intervene — before disease is established
51

All primordial and primary prevention measures are applicable here
Identification of high-risk groups enables targeted prophylaxis
Genetic counselling can alter host susceptibility

Q7. Primordial Prevention — discuss in detail. (May 2024) ★NEW★
(Refer Q5 of this topic for comprehensive coverage of primordial prevention in NCDs. Additional unique points
below.)
Additional Aspects
Historical Context:
Described by Strasser (1978) as the 4th level of prevention — added after Leavell & Clark's original 3
levels
Formally adopted by WHO in 1986 and reinforced in Ottawa Charter
Gained prominence in cardiovascular epidemiology — preventing risk factors for heart disease
Difference from Primary Prevention:
Aspect
Primordial Prevention
Primary Prevention
Target
Population — prevent risk factors
emerging
Susceptible individuals — prevent
disease
Timing
Before risk factors appear
After risk factors present, before
disease
Level
Societal/environmental
Individual/community
Example
Junk food tax (prevents obesity
risk factor)
Lifestyle counselling for obese
person

Q8. Differentiate between control, elimination and eradication. (2014) ★★★
Definitions and Comparison
1. Control
Definition: Reduction of disease incidence, prevalence, morbidity, or mortality to a locally acceptable level
Goal: Reduce disease burden; no specific endpoint — ongoing effort required
Agent: Remains in the environment/reservoir
Example: Malaria control in India — reduced cases but not eliminated
Methods: Vector control, chemoprophylaxis, case management
2. Elimination
Definition: Reduction to zero new cases in a defined geographic area (but pathogen/agent may still exist
elsewhere)
Requires sustained measures to prevent re-introduction
Two sub-types:
◦
Elimination of disease: Zero cases in region (e.g., India eliminated polio 2014, neonatal tetanus)
◦
Elimination of infection: Zero transmission in region
Examples in India: India eliminated guinea worm disease 2000, polio-free certification 2014
3. Eradication
Definition: Permanent reduction to zero of worldwide incidence + extinction of agent — NO intervention
needed thereafter
Most difficult — requires global action, highly specific tools (vaccine/control method)
52

Only ONE disease eradicated in history: Smallpox (1980, WHO)
Near-eradication: Guinea worm (dracunculiasis) — <15 cases globally (2023)
Criterion
Control
Elimination
Eradication
Incidence
Reduced
Zero in region
Zero globally
Agent exists?
Yes
Yes (elsewhere)
No (extinct)
Interventions
Continuous
Sustained surveillance
Not needed
Scope
Local/regional
National/regional
Global
Example
Malaria, TB
Polio (India), NNT
Smallpox

India-specific examples:
Eradicated: Smallpox (1977 last case in India; global 1980)
Eliminated: Polio (India certified polio-free March 2014); Guinea worm
Elimination target: Leprosy (grade 2 disability <1/million by 2030 — NLEP target)
Controlled: Malaria, Kala-azar (target: elimination by 2023 — ongoing), Lymphatic filariasis
53

5. DISASTER MANAGEMENT / BIOTERRORISM / TRIAGE

Q1. Define Disaster. Classify with Examples. Describe Phases of Disaster
Management. (2023, 2014) ★★★★★
Definition
A disaster is a serious disruption of the functioning of a community or society, causing widespread
human, material, economic, or environmental losses that exceed the ability of the affected community
to cope using its own resources (WHO / UNISDR definition).
Classification of Disasters
Category
Type
Examples
Natural
Geophysical
Earthquakes, Tsunamis, Volcanic eruptions
Natural
Meteorological
Cyclones, Floods, Droughts, Heatwaves
Natural
Biological
Epidemics, Pandemics (COVID-19, Plague)
Man-made /
Anthropogenic
Technological
Industrial accidents, Nuclear explosions
(Chernobyl, Bhopal gas tragedy)
Man-made /
Anthropogenic
Intentional / Conflict
Wars, Bioterrorism, Chemical warfare
Complex
Emergencies
Combined
Civil conflict + disease + displacement (e.g., Syrian
crisis)
Phases of Disaster Management (PPRR Model — 4 Phases)
Phase
Timing
Key Activities
1. Mitigation / Prevention
Before disaster
Risk mapping, Land-use planning, Building codes,
Public education, Vulnerability reduction
2. Preparedness
Before disaster
National Disaster Management Plan (NDMP), Mock
drills, Early warning systems, Stockpiling medicines &
equipment, Training of response teams, IEC activities
3. Response
During &
immediately after
Search & rescue (SAR), Triage, First aid, Evacuation,
Shelter, Food, Safe water, Surveillance for disease
outbreaks
4. Recovery /
Rehabilitation
Weeks–years
after
Restoration of infrastructure, Psychosocial support,
Livelihood restoration, Reconstruction, Long-term
surveillance
Indian Disaster Management Framework
Disaster Management Act 2005 — legal backbone
54

National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) — apex body under PM
State Disaster Management Authority (SDMA)
District Disaster Management Authority (DDMA)
National Disaster Response Force (NDRF) — 16 battalions
NDMP 2016 (updated 2019) — aligns with Sendai Framework 2015–2030
Exam Tip: Sendai Framework 4 Priorities: (i) Understanding risk, (ii) Strengthening governance, (iii)
Investing in DRR, (iv) Enhancing preparedness. Quote this in any disaster answer.
Q2. Public Health Significance of Bioterrorism & Man-Made Disasters. Add a Note
on Triage. (2010) ★★★★★
Bioterrorism — Definition
Intentional release of biological agents (bacteria, viruses, toxins) to cause disease, death, or terror in a
population.
CDC Classification of Bioterrorism Agents
Category
Features
Examples
A (Highest Priority)
High lethality, easy

Q1. Describe the Ecological Concept of Disease Causation — Epidemiological
Triad. (2022) ★★★★★
Ecological Concept of Disease Causation
Disease is not caused by a single factor; it results from a dynamic interaction between the host, agent,
and environment — the three components of the Epidemiological Triad (also called the
Epidemiological Triangle).
Components of the Epidemiological Triad
Component
Definition
Examples / Sub-types

The living organism (usually
human) in which the agent
causes disease
Demographic: Age, sex, race, occupation Biological:

status, concurrent illness Behavioural: Diet, smoking,
exercise, sexual behaviour

A factor whose presence (or

Biological: Bacteria, viruses, parasites, fungi (e.g.,
Mycobacterium tuberculosis) Chemical: Toxins,
drugs, heavy metals (e.g., lead) Physical: Radiation,
heat, trauma Nutritional: Deficiency (e.g., iodine →
goitre) or excess (obesity)

and agent

Biological: Vectors, reservoir hosts, sanitation Socio-
economic: Poverty, overcrowding, education, health
services
The Triad — Equilibrium and Disease
In health: Host, Agent, Environment are in dynamic equilibrium (balance)
Disease occurs when balance is disrupted in favour of the agent (e.g., increased virulence,
decreased host immunity, deteriorating environment)
Prevention targets can be identified for each vertex: host (vaccination), agent (antimicrobials),
environment (sanitation)
Application — Tuberculosis (Epidemiological Triad)

Mycobacterium tuberculosis — virulent, acid-fast bacillus, airborne droplet nuclei

overcrowding, age extremes, un-vaccinated (BCG)

60

Exam Tip: The Triad is most suitable for infectious diseases with a specific biological agent. For
chronic non-communicable diseases, Web of Causation is more appropriate — be ready to state this
distinction.
Q2. Discuss the Model of Web of Causation with Reference to CAD. (2015, 2011)

Web of Causation — Brian MacMahon (1960)
Proposed by Brian MacMahon & Pugh (1960), the Web of Causation acknowledges that diseases
(especially NCDs) result from a complex network of interacting causes rather than a single agent.
Key Concepts
Multiple causation: No single cause is sufficient or necessary for most diseases
Causal chain: Each cause itself has antecedent causes, creating a web-like network
Component cause: Any factor that contributes to the sufficient cause
Sufficient cause: Minimal combination of factors that inevitably produces disease
Necessary cause: A component present in every case (e.g., M. tuberculosis for TB)
Web of Causation for Coronary Artery Disease (CAD)
Level
Causal Factors
Distal / Societal causes
Urbanisation, Westernised lifestyle, Socioeconomic status, Cultural factors,
Globalisation
Intermediate causes
Physical inactivity, High-fat diet, Tobacco use, Psychosocial stress, Alcohol
Proximal / Biological
risk factors
Hypertension, Hyperlipidaemia (↑LDL, ↓HDL), Diabetes mellitus, Obesity
(central), Metabolic syndrome
Genetic / Host factors
Family history, Genetic polymorphisms (e.g., APOE genotype), Age (♂>45
yrs, ♀>55 yrs), Male sex
Pathological pathway
Endothelial injury → Atherosclerotic plaque → Plaque rupture → Thrombus
→ MI / Angina
Advantages of Web of Causation
Suitable for multifactorial diseases (CAD, diabetes, cancer)
Identifies multiple intervention points — breaking any strand weakens the web
Explains why risk factor reduction (e.g., BP control) reduces disease even without removing the
'root' cause
61

Limitations
Does not address socio-political root causes explicitly (unlike "Wheel of Causation")
Can be overly complex; difficult to quantify all strands
Lacks temporal sequencing of causes
Exam Tip: For NTRUHS: always draw a schematic web diagram conceptually in text — list factors at
societal → intermediate → proximal → biological levels. Examiner expects structured hierarchy.
Q3. Explain Epidemiological Triad in Relation to COVID-19 Infection. (2022)

Component
COVID-19 Specifics

AGENT — SARS-

CoV-2
RNA betacoronavirus; spike protein binds ACE-2 receptors; transmission:
respiratory droplets, aerosols (airborne), fomites (lesser); incubation 2–14
days (mean 5.1 days); multiple variants (Alpha, Delta, Omicron) with altered
transmissibility & immune escape
HOST — Human
All ages susceptible (highest severity in elderly, immunocompromised, co-
morbidities: DM, HTN, CKD, obesity) Immunity: Natural infection or vaccine-

higher risk; O — lower risk Behaviour: Mask compliance, social distancing,

ENVIRONMENT

Physical: Cold, dry indoor air → prolonged aerosol viability; crowded enclosed
spaces Biological: Animal reservoir (bats, pangolin); wet markets Socio-
economic: Urban density, poverty, healthcare access Health system: ICU
capacity, ventilators, PPE availability
Disease Control Strategies Mapped to Triad
Agent control: Antiviral drugs (Remdesivir, Paxlovid), Disinfection, Decontamination
Host protection: COVID-19 vaccination (PM COVID-19 vaccine drive), mask use, early
treatment, co-morbidity management
Environmental control: Ventilation, HEPA filtration, social distancing, lockdowns, travel
restrictions (IHR 2005 PHEIC declared Jan 2020)
Exam Tip: COVID-19 demonstrated the importance of all three triad components simultaneously —
highlighting why single-component interventions alone are insufficient.
Q4–Q5. Multifactorial Causation of Disease & Bradford Hill Criteria for Causality.

(2021, 2022) ★★★★

Multifactorial Causation — Rothman's Sufficient-Component Cause Model
Kenneth Rothman (1976) proposed the "Pie" model: disease is caused when a sufficient cause is
completed. Each sufficient cause is a 'pie' made up of component causes (pie slices). Removing any
component prevents that sufficient cause from being completed.
62

Bradford Hill Criteria for Causality (1965) — 9 Criteria
Criterion
Description
Example (Smoking & Lung
Cancer)
1. Strength of
Association
High RR/OR → stronger causal
inference
RR = 10–30 for heavy smokers vs
non-smokers
2. Consistency
Finding replicated in different
populations, times, by different
researchers
Confirmed across 29+ countries in
multiple studies
3. Specificity
One exposure → one specific
disease (less important for NCDs)
Smoking specifically associated
with squamous cell lung cancer
4. Temporality
Cause must precede effect — ONLY
essential criterion
Smoking precedes cancer by 20–
30 years
5. Biological Gradient
(Dose-Response)
↑ exposure → ↑ risk — supports
causation
Pack-years of smoking linearly
related to lung cancer risk
6. Plausibility
Biologically plausible mechanism
known
Carcinogens in tobacco (PAHs,
nitrosamines) → DNA mutation →
oncogenesis
7. Coherence
Association consistent with known
natural history and biology
Animal studies confirm tobacco
carcinogenesis
8. Experiment
Reduction in exposure reduces
disease risk
Smoking cessation → 50%
reduction in lung cancer risk over
10 years
9. Analogy
Similar cause-effect relationships
already established
Radiation causing cancer is
analogous
Exam Tip: Temporality is the ONLY sine qua non (essential) criterion among Bradford Hill's 9. Strength
of association is the most important but not essential. Memorise this — it is a favourite examiner
question.
Advanced Model / Triangle of Epidemiology (May 2025) — NEW
The Advanced Epidemiological Triangle expands the classic triad by adding a 4th component: the
Social/Structural Environment (also called the 'super-environment'). This acknowledges that
socioeconomic determinants (poverty, inequality, governance) operate above the classical environment
to influence all three triad vertices simultaneously.
Social determinants (WHO Commission — Marmot 2008): income, education, employment,
social support, early childhood development, structural violence
This model underpins the Social Determinants of Health (SDH) framework and the Health in All
Policies approach
Relevant to: NHP 2017, SDG 3 (Good Health & Well-being), Ayushman Bharat, Pradhan Mantri
Jan Arogya Yojana (PMJAY)

Greenhouse Effect
The Greenhouse Effect is the trapping of heat in the Earth's atmosphere by greenhouse gases
(GHGs), causing global warming. Solar radiation passes through the atmosphere, warms the Earth's
surface; re-emitted infrared radiation is absorbed by GHGs and re-radiated downward.

Role of Family in Health Promotion
13. Health behaviours: Diet, exercise habits, tobacco/alcohol use — all shaped primarily within
family
14. Immunisation: Family's awareness and acceptance determines child immunisation coverage
15. Antenatal care: Family support (especially mother-in-law) critical for ANC attendance in India
16. Mental health: Strong family ties protect against depression, suicidality; isolation increases risk
17. Elderly care: Geriatric health — joint family provides home-based care, reducing
institutionalisation
18. Child development: Early stimulation, nutrition, attachment within family determines long-term
health outcomes (1000 days concept)
Role of Family in Disease Causation
Genetic diseases: Haemophilia, sickle cell anaemia, thalassaemia — clustered in families
Infectious disease clustering: TB, typhoid, scabies, hepatitis — shared exposure within
household
Consanguineous marriages: Common in South India (Andhra Pradesh, Telangana) — ↑ risk of
autosomal recessive disorders
Family stressors: Domestic violence, poverty, broken families → ↑ mental illness, substance
abuse, child maltreatment
Role modelling of unhealthy behaviours: Parents who smoke → children more likely to smoke
Exam Tip: Family is the fundamental unit of epidemiology — the family pack concept (Cassel, 1972)
shows that social support from family modifies host resistance. Cite this for high-scoring answers.
70

Q2. Role of Family in Health and Disease. (Dec 2024)

NEW

(This question asks for a focused essay — cover both health-promoting and disease-causing roles of
family, incorporating recent evidence.)
Introduction: The family is universally recognised as the most fundamental social unit and primary
determinant of health. It acts simultaneously as a health-promoting institution (protective role) and a risk
environment (disease-causing role), depending on its structure, function, and dynamics.
A. Protective / Health-Promoting Role
Biological: Breastfeeding (↓ infant morbidity & mortality), family planning (birth spacing improves
maternal & child health)
Nutritional: Family food system — balanced diet, food security, home cooking vs processed
food
Psychological: Emotional support buffers stress response (HPA axis); strong family attachment
→ lower cortisol → better immune function
Care-seeking: Families identify illness early, facilitate health service utilisation, support
adherence to treatment
Socialisation: Transmission of positive health behaviours — handwashing, dental hygiene, sleep
habits
Economic safety net: Pooled family resources reduce catastrophic health expenditure
B. Disease-Causing Role
Shared exposure: Household TB transmission — secondary attack rate 10–15% in contacts
Genetic transmission: Autosomal recessive diseases (sickle cell, thalassaemia); X-linked
(haemophilia); autosomal dominant (Huntington's disease, familial hypercholesterolaemia)
Consanguinity: South Indian communities — increases homozygosity → recessive disease
expression
Domestic violence: IPV affects mental and physical health of women and children — GBV as a
public health crisis (WHO 2021: 1 in 3 women experience physical/sexual violence)
Dysfunctional family dynamics: Child abuse, neglect → adverse childhood experiences (ACEs)
→ lifelong health consequences (diabetes, depression, substance use — Felitti 1998 ACE
study)
Problem family: Family with persistent dysfunction (alcoholism, psychiatric illness, domestic
violence) — Park's definition
C. Family Medicine Approach
Family-centred care: ICMR recommends family physician as first point of contact under
Comprehensive Primary Health Care
Family health index (FHI): Tool to assess overall family health status for targeted interventions
Health & Wellness Centres (HWCs) under Ayushman Bharat — deliver comprehensive care to
family as a unit
Exam Tip: Dec 2024 is a recent paper — this is predicted to be a 15-mark essay. Open with definition,
cover protective + disease role systematically, close with public health implications for India.
71

Q3. Problem Family and Broken Family — Discuss in Detail. (2022) ★★★★
Problem Family (Park's Definition)
A problem family is one that fails to maintain a standard of health and social behaviour acceptable
to the community due to one or more of: mental deficiency, psychopathy, alcoholism, chronic ill-health,
social incapacity, domestic violence, or persistent poverty.
Characteristics of a Problem Family
Persistent poverty and economic instability despite available support
High rates of illness — disproportionate health service utilisation
Child neglect, abuse, malnutrition, poor school attendance
Domestic violence and antisocial behaviour
Social isolation — excluded from community networks
Substance abuse — alcoholism, drug dependence as central features
Broken Family
A broken family is one that has lost its structural integrity due to absence of one or both parents,
through: divorce/separation, death, desertion, imprisonment, long-term hospitalisation, or migration.
Health Consequences of Broken and Problem Families
Impact Area
Consequences
Child health
Malnutrition, recurrent infections, developmental delay, poor immunisation,
higher infant mortality
Mental health
Depression, anxiety, conduct disorder, suicidal behaviour in children and
adults; PTSD from domestic violence
Adolescent health
School dropout, early sexual debut, substance abuse, teenage pregnancy,
juvenile delinquency
Maternal health
Unsafe abortion, poor ANC, postnatal depression
Elderly
Neglect, elder abuse, institutionalisation, increased morbidity and mortality
(social isolation)
Public Health Interventions
Identification: ASHA workers / ANMs conduct household surveys to identify problem families for
targeted support
Family welfare services: Integrated Child Development Services (ICDS), POSHAN Abhiyaan
Legal protection: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005, Prohibition of Child
Marriage Act 2006
Counselling services: Family counselling centres under National Mental Health Programme

(NMHP)

Social support: Social welfare schemes — PM Awas Yojana, Aadhaar-linked cash transfers
72

Community-based rehabilitation: For substance abuse — de-addiction centres
Q4. Advantages and Disadvantages of Nuclear Family. (2023) ★★★
Advantages of Nuclear Family
Disadvantages of Nuclear Family
Greater individual autonomy — personal health
decisions without social pressure
Limited social support — single caregiver burnout
(especially mothers)
Better privacy — more open communication on
sensitive topics (sexual health, mental health)
No fallback during illness — no extended family
buffer
Lower risk of infectious disease clustering —
fewer household contacts
Elderly care crisis — nuclear families cannot
provide adequate geriatric care
Better family planning adoption — smaller family
norm more easily maintained
Child care deficit — daycare required; children
miss grandparent socialisation
Higher per-capita income allocation — more
resources per child (nutrition, education,
healthcare)
Psychological isolation — loneliness, increased
mental health disorders
Women's empowerment — greater role in
decision-making (financial, health)
Loss of cultural transmission — traditional health
practices, customs not passed on
Lower domestic violence incidence in healthy
nuclear families
Economic vulnerability — single income; one
illness → financial crisis
Exam Tip: Trend in India: Rapid nuclearisation of families — NFHS-5 (2019-21) data shows declining
household size (4.4 persons). This has major implications for elderly care, child nutrition, and mental
health — important context for exam answers.
Q5. Effects of Changes in Family Structure on Maternal and Child Health Care.
Contemporary Changes in Indian Family Structure
Transition from joint to nuclear families (urbanisation, migration, education, employment)
Rise of single-parent families (divorce, widowhood, migration of spouse)
Delay in marriage and childbearing — women's education and workforce participation
Declining household size — NFHS-5: Average household 4.4 persons (down from 5.5 in 1991)
Migration: Urban migration — male migrant workers → female-headed rural households
Effects on Maternal Health
Change
Effect on Maternal Health
Nuclearisation → loss of mother-
in-law support
Reduced ANC attendance (traditional gatekeeper absent);
inadequate postpartum care; postnatal depression ↑
Women in workforce
Better education → ↑ ANC utilisation, skilled birth attendance,
family planning adoption; BUT ↑ work stress
73

Late marriage/childbirth (>30 yrs)
↑ Gestational diabetes, hypertension, chromosomal abnormalities
(Down syndrome)
Single-parent family (female-
headed)
Economic hardship → poor nutrition, reduced ANC, higher MMR
risk
Male migration to cities
Women manage pregnancy without partner support → delayed
care-seeking, unsafe deliveries
Effects on Child Health
Loss of grandparent caregiving → reduced breastfeeding support; children in daycare → ↑
respiratory infections
Working mothers: ↓ exclusive breastfeeding duration; but ↑ income → better nutrition,
immunisation
Broken families: ↑ malnutrition, ↑ childhood mental health problems, poor school performance
Smaller families: ↑ per-child investment → better nutrition, immunisation, education
Consanguinity in traditional joint families: ↑ congenital malformations, recessive genetic
disorders
Policy Implications
Janani Suraksha Yojana (JSY) & Janani Shishu Suraksha Karyakram (JSSK): Compensate for
loss of family support by strengthening institutional delivery
POSHAN Abhiyaan: Community-based nutrition support substitutes for joint family nutritional
guidance
Pradhan Mantri Matru Vandana Yojana (PMMVY): Cash incentive for first live birth to
compensate for income loss during pregnancy
Anganwadi centres (ICDS): Substitute for extended family child-care support in nuclear family
settings
Exam Tip: NFHS-5 data (2019-21) is essential context: MMR 97/1,00,000 LB (SRS 2018-20), IMR
35.2/1,000 LB, U5MR 41.9. Use these figures to quantify the MCH burden when discussing family
structure changes.

— END OF GOLD-MEDAL ANSWER BANK: TOPICS 5–8 |

74
