subsections.title: 8. ROLE OF FAMILY IN HEALTH AND DISEASE
subsections.content:
Q1. Classify family. Broad functions of a family. Discuss its role in health and disease.

- (2022, 2021, 2019, 2018)

DEFINITION OF FAMILY

A family is a group of persons directly linked by kin connections, the adult members of which assume
responsibility for caring for children. (Park's Textbook, 27th ed.)

CLASSIFICATION OF FAMILY

A. Based on Structure:
- Nuclear Family: Husband, wife, and their unmarried children living together. Most common unit in urban
India.
- Joint/Extended Family: Three or more generations living together. Common in rural India; provides social
support.
- Three-generational Family: Grandparents, parents, and children.
- Single-parent Family: Only one parent with children (due to divorce, death, or abandonment).
- Reconstituted/Blended Family: Formed after remarriage.

B. Based on Marriage Type:
- Monogamous: One husband, one wife.
- Polygamous: Polygyny (one man, multiple wives); Polyandry (one woman, multiple husbands).

C. Based on Descent/Authority:
- Patriarchal: Authority with father; Matriarchal: Authority with mother.
- Patrilineal: Descent through father; Matrilineal: Descent through mother.

BROAD FUNCTIONS OF FAMILY

Function
Details
Biological / Reproductive
Continuation of species; regulation of sexual behavior; rearing of
children.
Economic
Unit of production and consumption; financial security for members.
Psychological
Emotional support, love, affection, sense of belonging; mental
health.
Educational / Socialization
Primary socialization; transmission of culture, values, norms to
children.
Protective / Welfare
Care during illness, old age; protection from external threats.
Religious
Transmission of religious beliefs, rituals, and practices.
Recreational
Leisure activities; family bonding.
Status Conferring
Assigns social position and role in society.

75

GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 3 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only

ROLE OF FAMILY IN HEALTH

Positive (Protective) Roles:
- Health Promotion: Families instill healthy behaviors — diet, hygiene, exercise, avoidance of
tobacco/alcohol.
- Preventive Care: Ensures immunization, antenatal care, family planning utilization.
- Early Diagnosis: Family members notice early signs of illness and facilitate healthcare seeking.
- Rehabilitation: Post-illness recovery and compliance with treatment is supported by family.
- Mental Health: Strong family bonds prevent depression, anxiety, substance abuse.
- Nutrition: Family determines dietary practices, breastfeeding, complementary feeding.

ROLE OF FAMILY IN DISEASE

Negative (Disease-promoting) Roles:
- Genetic Transmission: Hereditary diseases (hemophilia, thalassemia, diabetes) cluster in families.
- Communicable Disease Spread: Common source of infection for TB, enteric fever, COVID-19, hepatitis.
- Lifestyle Diseases: Unhealthy dietary habits, sedentary lifestyle, obesity shared within families.
- Domestic Violence & Abuse: Physical, sexual, emotional abuse causing physical & mental morbidity.
- Socioeconomic Deprivation: Low-income families face malnutrition, poor sanitation, limited health access.
- Problem Family: Dysfunctional family — social isolation, neglect, substance abuse, mental illness, poor
childcare.

CONCEPT OF PROBLEM FAMILY (Park)
A problem family is one that fails to maintain the health and social standards expected of a reasonable
household. Characteristics:
- Dirty, dilapidated home; poor housekeeping.
- Evidence of social incompetence: persistent poverty, debt, truancy, juvenile delinquency.
- Mental illness, alcoholism, or criminal history in family members.
- Repeated hospitalizations; multiple chronic illnesses.

  EXAM TIP: In 10-mark answers: Write Definition → Classification (with table) → Functions (table) → Role in
Health (5–6 points) → Role in Disease (5–6 points) → Conclusion. This structure ensures all marks.

  Q2. Role of family in health and disease.  ★★★★★  (Dec 2024 — NEW)
(Refer to Q1 above for comprehensive answer. Additional points for newer exam:)

ADDITIONAL CONTEMPORARY ASPECTS

- COVID-19 Pandemic: Family clustering of infection; family as quarantine unit; pandemic-related domestic
violence surge.
- Nuclear Family Trend: Urbanization and nuclearization reduces extended family support → increased
elderly neglect, increased institutional childcare need.
- Digital Health Literacy: Tech-savvy younger family members act as health navigators for elderly members.
- Family as Unit of Care: WHO emphasizes family-centered care in primary health care.

  Q3. Problem family and broken family — discuss in detail.  ★★★★  (2022)

PROBLEM FAMILY

76

GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 4 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only
Definition: A family that persistently fails to achieve a minimum standard of health, hygiene, and social
functioning expected from a reasonable household (J.H. Hutchinson, 1948).

Features of a Problem Family:
- Substandard housing — dirty, overcrowded, poorly maintained home.
- Inadequate nutrition — irregular meals, nutritional deficiencies in children.
- Financial irresponsibility — chronic indebtedness despite adequate income.
- Social incompetence — inability to use available social services.
- Mental or emotional disorders in one or more family members.
- History of alcoholism, drug abuse, or criminal behavior.
- Repeated child neglect, truancy, juvenile delinquency.

Health Implications of Problem Family:
- High infant mortality rate and malnutrition in children.
- Increased rates of communicable diseases due to poor hygiene.
- Higher rates of mental illness, depression, anxiety.
- Delayed immunization; non-compliance with treatment.
- Increased domestic violence; child abuse and neglect.

BROKEN FAMILY

Definition: A family in which the marital bond is disrupted due to death of a spouse, divorce, desertion, or
prolonged separation.

Types of Broken Family:
- Death of a spouse (widowhood).
- Divorce or legal separation.
- Desertion or abandonment.
- Imprisonment of a parent.
- Prolonged illness (physical/mental) causing functional separation.

Health Consequences:
- Children: Emotional insecurity, poor academic performance, behavioral problems, increased risk of
substance abuse.
- Single Parent: Overwork, financial stress, physical and mental exhaustion.
- Elderly: Social isolation, depression, neglect.
- Increased risk of child abuse and domestic violence.

  EXAM TIP: Differentiate: Problem family = functional failure (dysfunctional but intact). Broken family =
structural disruption (absent member). Examiners expect both compared.

  Q4. Advantages and disadvantages of nuclear family.  ★★★  (2023)

ADVANTAGES

DISADVANTAGES

Freedom from joint family conflicts and
interference.
Lack of social support during illness, old age,
pregnancy.
Better privacy; stronger marital bond.
No guidance from elders for child rearing.
77

GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 5 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only

ADVANTAGES

DISADVANTAGES

Economic independence and financial planning.
Elderly members face isolation and neglect.
Better reproductive health decisions — family
planning.
Children miss out on traditional values and
culture.
Reduced domestic disputes; lower stress.
Higher daycare costs; working mothers struggle.
Better child education — resources focused on
fewer children.
Single income dependency — financially
vulnerable.
Greater adaptability to urban lifestyle.
Mental health issues — loneliness, anxiety more
prevalent.

Indian Context: Rapid urbanization post-1991 liberalization has accelerated the shift from joint to nuclear
families. Health planners must account for reduced informal caregiving capacity in nuclear family households.

  Q5. Effects of changes in family structure on maternal and child health care.  ★★★  (2022)

CHANGING FAMILY STRUCTURES IN INDIA

- Shift from joint to nuclear families due to urbanization, industrialization, migration.
- Rise of single-parent families due to divorce, migration, conflict.
- Increasing number of dual-income households.
- Declining family size due to effective family planning.

EFFECTS ON MATERNAL HEALTH

- Reduced Antenatal Support: Nuclear families lack experienced elder women for guidance during
pregnancy.
- Decreased Breastfeeding: Working mothers in nuclear families have lower breastfeeding duration.
- Delayed Healthcare Seeking: No elders to recognize warning signs; single mother may delay seeking care.
- Mental Health: Increased postpartum depression in isolated nuclear mothers.
- Domestic Violence: Isolated mothers in nuclear families are more vulnerable.

EFFECTS ON CHILD HEALTH

- Nutrition: Without extended family knowledge, complementary feeding practices may be suboptimal.
- Immunization: Nuclear families dependent on healthcare system; no elder reminder system.
- Childcare: Dual-income families use institutional childcare — exposes children to infections.
- Psychological Development: Nuclear family children may have reduced social skills, increased screen time.
- Positive: Smaller family size → more resources per child → better nutrition, education outcomes.

  EXAM TIP: Structure as: Introduction → Changed family structures → Effect on Maternal Health (5 points) →
Effect on Child Health (5 points) → Public Health Implications → Conclusion.

78

GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 6 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only

subsections.title: 9. DIMENSIONS / DEFINITION OF HEALTH / POSITIVE HEALTH
subsections.content:
DIMENSIONS / DEFINITION OF HEALTH / POSITIVE

HEALTH

  Q1. Define health. Describe the various dimensions of health.  ★★★★★  (2022, 2019, 2018)

DEFINITIONS OF HEALTH

Source / Year
Definition

WHO, 1948

Health is a state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being
and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity.
WHO, 1984 (revised)
The extent to which an individual or group is able to realize
aspirations and satisfy needs and to change or cope with the
environment. Health is a resource for everyday life, not the object of
living.
Park (Social perspective)
Health is a condition and quality of the organism which expresses
an adequate functioning under given genetic and environmental
conditions.
Ecological view
Health is a dynamic equilibrium between man and his environment.
Biomedical model
Health is the absence of disease — reductionist, organ-focused.

DIMENSIONS OF HEALTH

Health is multidimensional. The major dimensions are:

1. Physical Dimension
- Perfect functioning of the body; all organs working optimally.
- Indicators: good complexion, body weight, posture; no disease or defect; high work capacity.

2. Mental Dimension
- Ability to think clearly, make rational decisions, cope with stress.
- Absence of mental illness; positive self-image; emotional stability.
- WHO definition of mental health: A state of well-being where individuals realize their potential, cope with
normal stress, work productively, and contribute to community.

3. Social Dimension
- Ability to maintain satisfying interpersonal relationships; participate in social activities.
- Social health implies harmony with family, community, and society.
- Indicators: Role fulfillment, social adaptability, community participation.

4. Spiritual Dimension
- Recognized by WHO in 1984 as important dimension.
- Sense of meaning, purpose, inner peace, and moral values in life.
- Resilience during adversity; ethical behavior.

5. Emotional Dimension
79

GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 7 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only
- Ability to express emotions appropriately; handle joy, sorrow, fear, anger constructively.
- Emotional health → better coping; reduces psychosomatic illnesses.

6. Vocational Dimension
- Ability to engage in productive, fulfilling work appropriate to one's abilities.
- Work gives meaning, income, and social identity.

7. Other Dimensions — as per Dunn's High Level Wellness:
- Intellectual: Capacity for learning, using information.
- Environmental: Living in clean, safe, supportive environment.
- Cultural: Health is shaped by cultural values, beliefs, practices.

SPECTRUM OF HEALTH (Leavell & Clark)
Health exists on a spectrum from optimal health → sub-clinical → clinical disease → death. Public health aims
to push the population toward the optimal end through primordial, primary, secondary, and tertiary prevention.

  EXAM TIP: In 10-mark questions: write 2 WHO definitions + table of dimensions (7 dimensions, each with
definition + indicator) + spectrum concept. Avoid listing only physical/mental/social — this fetches low marks.

  Q2. Explain the concept of Positive Health.  ★★★★  (2014)

CONCEPT OF POSITIVE HEALTH

Positive health is not merely freedom from disease but attainment of a state of physical, mental, and social well-
being at the highest possible level. It represents an active pursuit of health, not a passive state.

Key Features of Positive Health:
- Optimal functioning of body and mind, not just absence of disease.
- High physical fitness: strength, endurance, flexibility, coordination.
- Emotional stability: coping ability, positive outlook, resilience.
- Social integration: satisfying relationships; community participation.
- Spiritual well-being: sense of purpose, meaning, and ethical living.

DUNN'S HIGH LEVEL WELLNESS (1961)

H.L. Dunn coined 'High Level Wellness' as an integrated method of functioning oriented toward maximizing the
individual's potential within the environment. It has two axes:
- Health axis: from peak wellness to death.
- Environmental axis: from very favorable to very unfavorable.
The goal is to be in the 'High Level Wellness Zone' — peak wellness in a favorable environment.

POSITIVE HEALTH INDICATORS

Domain
Indicators
Physical
BMI 18.5–24.9; BP <120/80 mmHg; fasting blood glucose <100
mg/dL; no anemia.
Mental
Absence of mental illness; GHQ score; resilience; positive affect.
80

GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 8 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only
Domain
Indicators
Social
Social support networks; role satisfaction; participation in
community.
Functional
Work capacity; activities of daily living; quality of life (QOL)
measures.

POSITIVE HEALTH vs BIOMEDICAL MODEL
Biomedical model focuses on disease diagnosis and treatment (curative). Positive health model focuses on
enhancing well-being (promotive). Modern public health has shifted from disease prevention to health
promotion (Ottawa Charter, 1986).

  EXAM TIP: Link Positive Health to Ottawa Charter 1986 for Health Promotion: 5 action areas — Build healthy
public policy, Create supportive environments, Strengthen community action, Develop personal skills, Reorient
health services.

  Q3. What impact does Industrialization and Urbanization have on health?  ★★★★  (May 2024 —

NEW)

INDUSTRIALIZATION AND HEALTH

Adverse Effects:
- Occupational Hazards: Pneumoconiosis, silicosis, byssinosis, noise-induced hearing loss, chemical toxicity.
- Environmental Pollution: Air, water, soil pollution from industrial effluents → respiratory diseases, cancers.
- Lifestyle Changes: Sedentary work, stress, shift work → cardiovascular disease, diabetes, mental illness.
- Child Labor & Exploitation: Child labor in industries → injury, malnutrition, denied education.
- Social Disruption: Migration; weakening of family bonds; urban slums.

Positive Effects:
- Economic Growth: Increased income → better nutrition, housing, healthcare access.
- Medical Technology: Industrial development drives pharmaceutical and diagnostic advances.
- Infrastructure: Roads, electricity, sanitation improve with industrialization.

URBANIZATION AND HEALTH

Adverse Effects:
- Slum Formation: 40–50% of urban population in India lives in slums — overcrowding, poor sanitation.
- Water & Sanitation: Inadequate safe water → diarrhea, typhoid, cholera.
- Air Pollution: Vehicular and industrial emissions → ARI, COPD, asthma, lung cancer.
- Urban Heat Islands: Higher temperatures → heat stress, vector breeding.
- Mental Health: Isolation, anonymity, poverty, violence → depression, anxiety, substance abuse.
- Non-Communicable Diseases: Sedentary lifestyle, fast food, stress → hypertension, diabetes, obesity.
- Road Traffic Accidents: Increased motorization → leading cause of trauma in urban India.

Positive Effects:
- Better access to hospitals, specialists, diagnostic services.
- Higher female literacy and employment → better reproductive health.
- Improved water supply and sanitation systems in planned urban areas.
81

GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 9 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only

PUBLIC HEALTH RESPONSE

- National Urban Health Mission (NUHM): Addresses healthcare needs of urban poor.
- Smart Cities Mission: Promotes healthy urban environments.
- Pollution Control Boards: Regulate industrial and vehicular emissions.

  EXAM TIP: This NEW question (May 2024) — structure as: Introduction → Industrialization adverse/positive
→ Urbanization adverse/positive → SDG linkage → Conclusion. Mentioning NUHM and NCAP (National Clean
Air Programme) adds marks.

  Q4. Give an account of changing concepts in Public Health.  ★★★  (2018, 2015)

The concept of public health has evolved through several phases:

Era / Period
Concept & Focus
Miasma Theory (pre-1850)
Disease caused by 'bad air' (miasma). Focus on environmental
sanitation — drainage, sewage.
Germ Theory (1850–1900)
Pasteur, Koch — specific microorganisms cause specific diseases.
Focus on infection control, vaccines.
Epidemiological Triad (early
20th c.)
Host–Agent–Environment interaction. Multifactorial causation
recognized.
Social Medicine (1940s–50s)
Health determined by social and economic factors. Virchow:
'Medicine is a social science.'
Biomedical Model (1950s–70s)
Dominant curative model; organ/disease-focused; hospital-centered
care.
Comprehensive PHC (1978)
Alma Ata Declaration: 'Health for All by 2000.' Primary Health Care
as cornerstone.
Health Promotion (1986)
Ottawa Charter: Health as resource for everyday life.
Empowerment, policy, environment.
Sustainable Development
(2000s–)
MDGs → SDGs. Health as integral to development. SDG 3: Good
Health and Well-being.
Universal Health Coverage

(UHC)

Ensuring all people have access to quality health services without
financial hardship.
Planetary Health (2020s)
One Health, climate change, antimicrobial resistance as health
threats.

  EXAM TIP: Conclude with: 'The concept has evolved from a negative (absence of disease) to a positive
(optimal well-being), from individual to community, from biomedical to socioecological model.' This theoretical
framing earns distinction marks.

  Q5. Discuss — 'Health is not Stethoscope and Pills'.  ★★★  (2011)

82

GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 10 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only
This statement critiques the biomedical reductionist model of health, which equates health with clinical
diagnosis (stethoscope) and pharmacological treatment (pills).

LIMITATIONS OF THE BIOMEDICAL MODEL

- Focuses on disease rather than health promotion.
- Neglects social, psychological, environmental determinants of health.
- Hospitalo-centric and curative in orientation — addresses only 20% of health burden.
- Fails to address root causes: poverty, illiteracy, malnutrition, poor sanitation.

WHAT HEALTH TRULY REQUIRES

- Social Determinants: Income, education, employment, housing, food security.
- Primary Prevention: Immunization, sanitation, safe water, health education.
- Community Participation: Empowered communities take ownership of health.
- Intersectoral Action: Agriculture, education, infrastructure, finance — all impact health.
- Mental & Spiritual Well-being: Not addressed by any pill or stethoscope.

McKeown Thesis: The major decline in infectious disease mortality in England (18th–19th century) occurred
BEFORE the introduction of vaccines and antibiotics — attributed to better nutrition, sanitation, and living
conditions. This proves health is more than medicine.

  EXAM TIP: Strong answers quote McKeown Thesis + Virchow ('Medicine is social science; politics is nothing
but medicine on a large scale') + WHO 1984 revised definition. These are gold medal differentiators.

83

GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 11 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only

subsections.title: 10. COLD CHAIN / IMMUNIZATION / VACCINES / AEFI / SURVEILLANCE
subsections.content:
COLD CHAIN / IMMUNIZATION / VACCINES / AEFI /

SURVEILLANCE

  Q1. Define cold chain. Describe cold chain equipment and maintenance. What is AEFI?

- (2022, 2021, 2018)

DEFINITION: COLD CHAIN

Cold chain is the system of storage and transport of vaccines at recommended temperatures (between +2°C
and +8°C for most vaccines; -15°C to -25°C for OPV) from the point of manufacture to the point of
administration, to maintain their potency and efficacy.

WHY COLD CHAIN IS CRITICAL

- Vaccines are heat-labile biological products — exposure to heat irreversibly destroys potency.
- Some vaccines (OPV) are also sensitive to freezing — freeze-sensitive vaccines include DPT, TT, Hep-B,
DT.
- Failure of cold chain is a major cause of vaccine failure and re-emergence of vaccine-preventable diseases.

COLD CHAIN EQUIPMENT AT EACH LEVEL

Level
Equipment
National / State
Cold rooms (+2°C to +8°C) and Frozen rooms (-15°C to -25°C).
Capacity: months of supply.
Regional / District
Deep Freeze (-15°C to -25°C) for OPV; Ice-Lined Refrigerators
(ILR) for other vaccines. Refrigerated vans for transport.
CHC / PHC Level
ILR (Ice-Lined Refrigerator) — maintains temp even during power
failure for 6–8 hrs. Deep freezer for OPV.
Sub-centre / Village
Vaccine Carrier (4 icepacks; maintains 4°C for 12 hrs). Cold Box
(20–22 icepacks; up to 48–72 hrs).
Health Worker Level
Vaccine Carrier with icepacks. Hub cutter for syringe destruction.

ICE-LINED REFRIGERATOR (ILR) — KEY FACTS

- Lined with ice-water mixture along the walls.
- Temperature maintained at +2°C to +8°C.
- Maintains cold for 6–8 hours after power failure — critical for power-deficit areas.
- Top-opening design prevents cold air escape when door opened.

VACCINE VIAL MONITOR (VVM)

A small adhesive label on vaccine vial with a heat-sensitive inner square:
- Stage 1: Inner square lighter than outer ring — USABLE.
- Stage 2: Inner square = outer ring colour — USABLE but use first.
- Stage 3: Inner square darker than outer ring — DO NOT USE.
- Stage 4: Inner square very dark — DISCARD immediately.
84

GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 12 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only

COLD CHAIN MAINTENANCE

- Daily: Check temperature twice daily; record in temperature log chart; ensure proper stacking.
- Weekly: Defrost ILR when ice layer >0.5 cm (reduces efficiency).
- Monthly: Stock inventory, expiry date check, VVM inspection.
- Yearly: Annual maintenance contract (AMC) for cold chain equipment.
- General: No vaccines in door shelves; use basket/wire rack; cold box with icepacks for transport; FIFO
(First In First Out) principle.

WHAT IS AEFI?

AEFI = Adverse Event Following Immunization.
Definition: Any untoward medical occurrence which follows immunization and which does not necessarily have
a causal relationship with the usage of the vaccine. (WHO, 1999 — Glossary of Vaccine Safety Terminology.)

CLASSIFICATION OF AEFI (WHO 2012 revised)
Category
Description / Examples
Vaccine product-related
Caused by intrinsic properties of vaccine. Vaccine-associated
paralytic polio (VAPP) from OPV.
Vaccine quality defect-related
Improper manufacture — clumping, wrong concentration. Sterility
failure.
Immunization error-related
(programmatic)
Wrong dose, site, technique; contamination; reconstitution error;
cold chain failure.
Immunization anxiety-related
Vasovagal syncope, hyperventilation. Not caused by vaccine itself.
Coincidental
Temporal association only; would have occurred regardless of
vaccination. SIDS following vaccine.

COMMON AEFIs AND MANAGEMENT

AEFI

Management
Local reaction (pain, swelling,
redness)
Cold compress; analgesics; usually self-limiting.
Fever post-immunization
Paracetamol; monitor; usually <38.5°C and resolves in 24–48 hrs.
Anaphylaxis
Adrenaline 1:1000 IM (0.01 mg/kg); IV fluids; hospitalize.
Persistent crying (>3 hrs) —
DPT
Monitor; usually resolves; next dose use DT.
Febrile convulsion
Antipyretics; if recurrent — consult neurologist.
Vaccine-Associated Paralytic
Polio
Notify; shift to IPV; supportive rehabilitation.

  EXAM TIP: AEFI classification (5 categories per WHO 2012) is frequently asked. Learn: Vaccine product →
Quality defect → Immunization error → Anxiety-related → Coincidental. Use this order in exams for maximum
marks.

85

GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 13 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only
  Q2. Classify Vaccines. Describe National Immunization Schedule.  ★★★★★  (2018)

CLASSIFICATION OF VACCINES

A. Based on Nature:
Type
Examples
Live Attenuated
BCG, OPV, MMR, Varicella, Yellow Fever, Rotavirus. Elicit strong,
long-lasting immunity.
Inactivated (Killed)
IPV, Hepatitis A, Rabies (HDCV), Pertussis component of DPT.
Toxoids
TT (Tetanus Toxoid), Diphtheria Toxoid. Formaldehyde-inactivated
toxins.
Subunit / Component
Hepatitis B (HBsAg), Acellular Pertussis (aP), Typhoid Vi antigen.
Conjugate Vaccines
Hib conjugate, MenACWY, PCV13. Polysaccharide linked to
protein carrier.
Recombinant DNA
Hepatitis B, HPV (Gardasil, Cervarix).
mRNA Vaccines
COVID-19 (Pfizer, Moderna). New platform.

B. Based on Usage:
- Universal Immunization Programme (UIP) vaccines vs. Special situation vaccines.
- Monovalent vs. Polyvalent (e.g., OPV — trivalent; nOPV2 — monovalent).

NATIONAL IMMUNIZATION SCHEDULE (NIS) — INDIA 2023

Age
Vaccines

(birth dose, 0.5 ml IM thigh)

  EXAM TIP: PCV (Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccine) was added to UIP in 2017. Rotavirus vaccine expanded
nationally. Always mention these in NIS — differentiates answers. JE vaccine: given only in 112 endemic
districts.

86

GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 14 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only
  Q3. Surveillance of AEFI — describe in detail.  ★★★★★  (May 2025 — NEW)

OBJECTIVES OF AEFI SURVEILLANCE

- Detect and investigate AEFIs promptly to identify cause (vaccine, program, or coincidental).
- Ensure vaccine safety and maintain public confidence in immunization.
- Identify immunization errors and strengthen program quality.
- Provide data for regulatory decisions on vaccine licensing.

AEFI SURVEILLANCE SYSTEM IN INDIA

India follows the WHO-recommended AEFI Surveillance framework, operational under the Universal
Immunization Programme (UIP) and overseen by MoHFW.

Key Components:
- Case Detection: Health workers, caregivers, clinicians report AEFIs.
- Reporting: Online reporting through Co-WIN portal and AEFI reporting forms.
- Investigation: District AEFI Committee investigates serious/cluster AEFIs.
- Causality Assessment: State AEFI Committee and National AEFI Committee.
- Feedback & Action: Findings shared back to field; corrective action taken.

AEFI REPORTING CRITERIA (Serious AEFIs must be reported within 24–48 hrs)
Category
Report Timeline
Serious AEFI (hospitalization,
death, life-threatening)
Within 24 hours to district/state.
Cluster AEFI (≥2 cases same
session/batch)
Immediate — same day.
Non-serious AEFI (local
reactions, minor fever)
Within 7 days, in monthly tally.

AEFI INVESTIGATION PROCESS

1. Case Identification: History, clinical examination, onset timing after vaccination.
2. Lab Investigation: Where needed — blood culture, CSF, histopathology.
3. Causality Assessment: Using WHO 2012 classification algorithm.
4. Report Preparation: AEFI investigation form completed; batch number, storage checked.
5. Corrective Action: If immunization error found → training, supervision, cold chain review.
6. Regulatory Report: Pharmacovigilance — ADR reporting to CDSCO (Central Drugs Standard Control
Organisation).

CAUSALITY ASSESSMENT — WHO 2012 Algorithm
Step 1: Characterize the event (clinical diagnosis, timeline).
Step 2: Review evidence (biologic plausibility, similar cases in literature).
Step 3: Classify:
Classification
Meaning
Consistent causal association
Evidence supports vaccine → AEFI.
87

GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 15 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only
Classification
Meaning
Inconsistent causal association
(coincidental)
Evidence favors another cause.
Indeterminate
Evidence insufficient to attribute or deny causal link.
Unclassifiable
Insufficient evidence to classify.

NATIONAL LEVEL — AEFI COMMITTEE STRUCTURE

- Health Worker Level: Reports AEFI; provides first aid.
- District AEFI Committee: Chaired by CMHO; investigates serious AEFIs; submits report to state.
- State AEFI Committee: Expert committee; causality assessment for serious AEFIs.
- National AEFI Committee (NAEFI): Ministry level; reviews national trends; advises regulatory action.

PHARMACOVIGILANCE LINKAGE

- AEFI data feeds into Pharmacovigilance Programme of India (PvPI) under CDSCO.
- Adverse Drug Reaction (ADR) Monitoring Centres across India collect vaccine safety data.
- WHO Collaborating Centre for Adverse Reaction Monitoring: Uppsala Monitoring Centre (UMC), Sweden.

  EXAM TIP: NEW question (May 2025) — examiners expect: Objectives → Components of surveillance →
Reporting criteria (with timeline table) → Investigation process (numbered) → Causality assessment (WHO
2012) → Committee structure → Pharmacovigilance. This comprehensive structure earns gold medal marks.

  Q4. Describe principles of Immunization. What diseases have been eradicated and
eliminated?  ★★★★  (2021)

PRINCIPLES OF IMMUNIZATION

A. Immunological Principles:
- Primary Response: First exposure → lag phase → antibody production (IgM first, then IgG). Slow but
establishes immunological memory.
- Secondary (Anamnestic) Response: Re-exposure → rapid, large, sustained antibody production (IgG).
Basis of booster doses.
- Herd Immunity: When a critical proportion of population is immune, even non-immune individuals are
protected. Threshold varies by R0 of disease.
Herd immunity threshold = 1 − (1/R0). For measles (R0=12–18): ~92–95% coverage needed.

B. Practical Principles:
- Age: Given at optimal immunological age — not too young (maternal antibodies interfere) or too late
(disease risk window missed).
- Schedule: Multiple doses needed for some vaccines (e.g., OPV) to ensure adequate response despite
interference.
- Route and Dose: Oral (OPV), Intradermal (BCG), Intramuscular (DPT, Hep B), Subcutaneous (MR). Wrong
route → failure or adverse reaction.
- Cold Chain: Essential for heat-labile vaccines to maintain potency.
- Simultaneous Administration: Multiple vaccines at same visit do not interfere — recommended by WHO.

ERADICATED DISEASES

88

GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 16 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only
Disease
Year Eradicated / Details
Smallpox
Globally eradicated in 1980. Last natural case in Somalia, 1977.
Certified by WHO. Only human disease fully eradicated.
Rinderpest
Animal disease. Eradicated in 2011. Relevant to One Health
concept.

ELIMINATED / NEAR-ELIMINATION

Disease
Status
Polio (WPV)
India certified polio-free in 2014. Globally — only WPV1 in Pakistan
and Afghanistan as of 2025.
Guinea Worm
Near eradication (<10 cases/year globally).
Maternal & Neonatal Tetanus
India eliminated MNT in 2015.
Leprosy
India eliminated (<1/10,000) in 2005. Sub-national elimination
ongoing.
Yaws
India declared yaws-free in 2016.
Measles
Not yet eliminated — India targets elimination by 2026.

  EXAM TIP: Distinguish: Eradication (global, permanent, no intervention needed) vs Elimination (regional,
ongoing surveillance needed) vs Control (reduction in incidence). Smallpox = only eradicated human disease.
This distinction always gets extra marks.

  Q5. Immunization in Primary Health Care.  ★★★  (2015)

PRIMARY HEALTH CARE AND IMMUNIZATION

Alma Ata Declaration (1978) identified immunization against the major infectious diseases as one of the 8
essential components of PHC. UIP (Universal Immunization Programme) is the backbone of immunization
delivery through PHC in India.

UIP — STRUCTURE AND DELIVERY

- Launched: 1985 (Expanded Programme on Immunization started 1978 by WHO).
- Coverage target: >90% for all antigens in all districts.
- Delivery points: Sub-centres, PHCs, outreach sessions, Urban Health Posts.
- Healthcare worker: ANM (Auxiliary Nurse Midwife) — primary vaccinator at village level.

KEY STRATEGIES FOR PHC-BASED IMMUNIZATION

- Routine Immunization: Fixed-site and outreach sessions at sub-centre level; ANM-led.
- Pulse Polio Immunization (PPI): Mass campaigns on National Immunization Days (NIDs) — house-to-
house follow-up.
- Mission Indradhanush: Launched 2014; targets children missed by routine immunization; >90% coverage
goal.
- Intensified Mission Indradhanush (IMI 5.0): Ongoing; focuses on zero-dose children and underserved
populations.
89

GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 17 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only
- Electronic Vaccine Intelligence Network (eVIN): Real-time cold chain monitoring and vaccine stock
management.
- Co-WIN Platform: Digital vaccine tracking, beneficiary management (originally for COVID-19; now
expanded).

CHALLENGES IN PHC IMMUNIZATION

- Cold chain breaks in power-deficit rural areas.
- Vaccine hesitancy — misinformation, cultural beliefs.
- Hard-to-reach populations: Tribal, migrant, urban slum dwellers.
- Shortage of ANMs; high absenteeism.
- Drop-out rates between doses (DPT1 to DPT3).

INDICATORS OF IMMUNIZATION COVERAGE

Indicator
Formula / Definition
Immunization Coverage
(Vaccinated / Target) × 100. Target >90%.
Drop-out Rate
[(DPT1 − DPT3) / DPT1] × 100. Target <10%.
Effective Coverage
Coverage × Vaccine Efficacy.
Zero-dose children
Children who have not received even one dose of any routine
vaccine.

  EXAM TIP: Mentioning Mission Indradhanush, eVIN, and zero-dose children in this answer differentiates
distinction from pass answers. Conclude: 'Immunization is the most cost-effective public health intervention —
$1 invested yields $44 in returns (WHO, 2019).'

90

subsections.content:
SURVEILLANCE — Types, NCD Surveillance & AEFI
Surveillance

Q1. Types of Surveillance with Examples  ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Oct 2025 — NEW)
Definition
Surveillance is the continuous, systematic collection, analysis, interpretation, and dissemination of
health data for planning, implementation, and evaluation of public health programmes. (WHO)
- Mnemonic: "C-A-I-D" — Collect, Analyse, Interpret, Disseminate
A. Based on Source of Data
Type
Description
Example
Passive
Routine data from health facilities; least effort
OPD registers, death
certificates
Active
Active search by investigators; more accurate
House-to-house polio
surveys
Sentinel
Selected sentinel sites represent whole
population
Sentinel STI/HIV clinics
Syndromic
Based on symptom clusters; early warning
Fever with rash surveillance

B. Based on Coverage
Universal / Comprehensive: Entire population covered. Example: Civil Registration of Births &
Deaths
Sample / Sentinel: Representative sample. Example: National Family Health Survey (NFHS)
C. Based on Objectives
Epidemiological Surveillance: Monitor disease trends; detect outbreaks (e.g., Dengue
surveillance)
Laboratory Surveillance: Pathogen typing & drug resistance (e.g., AMR surveillance)
Nutritional Surveillance: Monitor nutritional status of vulnerable groups (e.g., SAM tracking)
Demographic Surveillance: Births, deaths, migration — DSS sites
Environmental Surveillance: Water, air quality monitoring; polio AFP surveillance
D. Special Types
Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme (IDSP): India's national surveillance network — P
(peripheral), L (laboratory), S (specialist) forms
Event-Based Surveillance (EBS): Real-time monitoring of unusual health events, rumours via
media scanning
113

- High Yield: WHO's 'Best Buys' for NCD control rely on STEPS surveillance data. 25×25 target =
25% reduction in premature NCD mortality by 2025.
- Mnemonic: STEPS: Questionnaire → Physical → Biochemical (1→2→3)

Q3. Surveillance of AEFI (Adverse Events Following Immunization)
- (May 2025 — NEW)
Definition — AEFI
AEFI is any untoward medical occurrence which follows immunization and which does not necessarily
have a causal relationship with the usage of the vaccine. (WHO definition)
- Mnemonic: "Any Medical Occurrence Post-Immunization" — does NOT imply causation
Classification of AEFI (WHO 2013 Brighton Collaboration)
Category
Cause
Example
Vaccine product-related
Inherent property of vaccine

OPV — VAPP

Vaccine quality defect
Manufacturing defect
Aggregated particles →
fever
Immunization error
Wrong dose, site, technique
Abscess at injection site
Immunization anxiety
Fear/anxiety about injection
Vasovagal syncope
Coincidental
Unrelated — would have occurred anyway
Febrile seizures (URTI)

AEFI Surveillance System in India
Legal basis: Drugs & Cosmetics Act; National Pharmacovigilance Programme (PvPI)
Nodal body: Ministry of Health & Family Welfare; National AEFI Committee
Levels: Health worker → Block/PHC → District → State → National AEFI Committee
Reporting Criteria — Which AEFIs to Report?
ALL serious AEFIs must be reported within 24–48 hours:
Serious AEFI includes: Death, hospitalisation, life-threatening events, permanent disability,
anaphylaxis, encephalopathy, VAPP, seizures, sepsis at injection site, abscess
Non-serious AEFIs (fever, redness, pain) → record in health register; no immediate upward reporting
needed
Investigation of AEFI
1. Verify the event  →  2. Collect patient & vaccine details  →  3. Causality assessment  →  4. Report to
AEFI Committee  →  5. Corrective action
Causality Assessment: WHO causality categories: Consistent causal association / Inconsistent /
Indeterminate / Unclassifiable
Key Committees
District AEFI Committee: Chaired by CMO/CMHO; investigates all serious AEFIs
State AEFI Committee: Reviews district reports; provides technical guidance
National AEFI Committee: Apex body; issues national alerts; publishes annual report
114

- High Yield: Anaphylaxis — most severe AEFI. Occurs within 30 min. Adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg IM
is treatment. Every vaccination point must have adrenaline.
- Mnemonic: AEFI Classes: "VP-QE-IA-Anxiety-Coin" — Vaccine Product, Quality, Error,
Anxiety, Coincidental

Q4. Herd Immunity and its Role in Infection Control  ⭐⭐⭐ (Nov 2023 — NEW)
Definition
Herd immunity (community immunity / population immunity) is the indirect protection afforded to
susceptible individuals when a sufficiently large proportion of a population has become immune to an
infection — either through vaccination or prior infection — thereby reducing the likelihood of infection
for individuals who lack immunity.
Mechanism
When immune individuals act as 'barriers', the chain of transmission is broken. Even non-immune
individuals are protected because the pathogen cannot easily find new susceptible hosts.
- Mnemonic: "Sea of immune, island of susceptible — infection can't cross the sea"
Herd Immunity Threshold (HIT)
HIT = proportion of population that must be immune to halt epidemic spread.
Formula: HIT = 1 − (1/R₀)
Where R₀ = Basic Reproduction Number (average secondary cases from 1 primary case in fully
susceptible population)
Disease
R₀
HIT (approx.)
Measles

12–18

92–95%

Polio
5–7

80–85%

COVID-19 (original)
2–3

~67%

Smallpox
5–7
83%
Mumps
4–7

75–86%

Role in Infection Control
Protects vulnerable groups: Neonates, immunocompromised, elderly, pregnant women who
cannot be vaccinated
Outbreak prevention: High coverage prevents explosive outbreaks — seen with smallpox
eradication
Endemic control: Sustained high coverage maintains endemicity below epidemic threshold
Programme target: Vaccination programmes aim to exceed HIT to achieve herd immunity
Achieving Herd Immunity — Methods
Vaccination: PREFERRED — safe, ethical, no morbidity. Pulse Polio, measles campaigns
Natural infection: NOT recommended — leads to morbidity, mortality, and risk of more virulent
strains
Limitations
115

- Herd immunity does NOT apply to non-communicable diseases or non-person-to-person infections
(e.g., tetanus, rabies)
- Vaccine hesitancy creates susceptible pockets → outbreaks (measles resurgence in Europe 2018)
- Waning immunity may reduce herd effect over time
- Antigenic drift (influenza, COVID variants) may evade existing immunity
- High Yield: Measles requires highest HIT (~95%) due to very high R₀. That is why 2 doses of
MMR are given in UIP.
- Mnemonic: HIT = 1 − 1/R₀  ||  Higher R₀ → Higher HIT needed

subsections.title: 14. SCREENING
subsections.content:
SCREENING — Applications, Criteria, Advantages &
Disadvantages

Q1. Define Screening. Uses, Criteria and Validity of Screening Tests

- (2023)

Definition
Screening is the presumptive identification of unrecognized disease or defect by the application of
tests, examinations or other procedures that can be applied rapidly to sort out apparently healthy
individuals who probably have a disease from those who probably do not. (Commission on Chronic
Illness, USA, 1951)
KEY: Screening does NOT diagnose; it sorts into presumptively positive and presumptively negative
groups.
Types of Screening
Mass screening: Whole population screened regardless of risk (e.g., neonatal heel-prick for PKU)
Selective / High-risk: Target high-risk groups (e.g., smokers for lung cancer)
Multiphasic: Multiple tests in one visit (e.g., health camp — BP, CBG, BMI together)
Opportunistic: Screening during unrelated clinical visit (e.g., BP at dental clinic)
Case finding: Physician-initiated on individual patients
Uses of Screening
1. Early detection of disease at preclinical stage (→ better prognosis)
2. Reduction of disease burden at population level
3. Cost-effective intervention when treatment at early stage is cheaper
4. Epidemiological research — measure prevalence, natural history
5. Evaluation of prevention programmes
Wilson & Jungner Criteria (WHO 1968) — 10 Classic Criteria
- Mnemonic: "SITE-SAFE-TEC" mnemonic or simply WHO 10 criteria
#
Criterion
Details
1
Important health
problem
Common / severe disease with significant burden
116

2
Accepted treatment
available
No point detecting if no treatment exists
3
Facilities for
diagnosis & treatment
Infrastructure must exist
4
Recognizable
latent/early stage
Natural history must include detectable preclinical phase
5
Suitable test exists
Sensitive, specific, acceptable, affordable
6
Test acceptable to
population
Non-invasive / minimally invasive; culturally acceptable
7
Natural history
understood
Epidemiology and biology of disease must be known
8
Agreed policy on
treatment
Standard treatment protocols must exist
9
Cost-effective
Cost of screening < cost of treating late disease
10
Continuous process
Not a one-time exercise; ongoing programme

Validity of Screening Tests
Validity = ability of a test to measure what it is intended to measure. Assessed by:
Measure
Formula
Ideal Value
Sensitivity
TP / (TP + FN) × 100  [True Positive Rate]
High (>80%)
Specificity
TN / (TN + FP) × 100  [True Negative Rate]
High (>80%)
PPV
TP / (TP + FP) × 100 — depends on prevalence
Varies
NPV

TN / (TN + FN) × 100

Varies
Accuracy
(TP + TN) / Total × 100
High

Sensitivity vs Specificity trade-off: Increasing sensitivity → decreasing specificity (and vice
versa). The cut-off is chosen based on programme priorities.
Youden's Index: Sensitivity + Specificity − 1  (max = 1.0; best cut-off)
ROC Curve: Receiver Operating Characteristic — plots Sensitivity vs (1−Specificity); larger AUC =
better test
- High Yield: Sensitivity is used for RULING OUT disease (SnNout); Specificity for RULING IN
disease (SpPin).
- Mnemonic: "SnNout" — High Sensitivity, Negative result rules OUT. "SpPin" — High
Specificity, Positive result rules IN.

Q2. Application of Screening for Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs)
- (Oct 2025 — NEW)
Rationale for NCD Screening
117

NCDs have a long latent period, are usually asymptomatic early, and have effective interventions when
detected early — making them ideal candidates for screening programmes.
Major NCD Screening Programmes in India — NPCDCS
National Programme for Prevention & Control of Cancer, Diabetes, CVD & Stroke (NPCDCS)
mandates population-based screening for:
NCD
Target Group
Screening Test
Cut-off
Hypertension
≥30 yrs
BP measurement
≥140/90 mmHg
Diabetes Mellitus
≥30 yrs

FBS / RBS

FBS ≥126 / RBS ≥200

mg/dL
Cervical Cancer
Women 30–65 yrs
VIA / Pap smear / HPV
DNA
VIA — acetowhite lesion
Breast Cancer
Women ≥30 yrs
CBE, Mammography
Palpable lump /
radiological

yrs

Special NCD Screening Programmes
Neonatal screening: Congenital hypothyroidism (TSH), PKU, sickle cell — heel-prick at 48–72 hrs
Antenatal screening: Gestational DM (GCT/OGTT), pre-eclampsia (BP, urine protein), anaemia
(Hb), HIV, syphilis
School health screening: Vision, hearing, dental, growth, scoliosis, skin
Occupational screening: Audiometry for noise-exposed workers, pulmonary function for miners
Effectiveness of NCD Screening
Evaluated by: ↓ in disease-specific mortality | ↓ stage at diagnosis | ↑ 5-year survival | Cost per
QALY/DALY averted
- High Yield: VIA (Visual Inspection with Acetic Acid) is WHO-recommended for cervical cancer
screening in LMICs — cheap, no lab needed, same-visit treatment possible ('see & treat').
- Mnemonic: NCD BCHOD: BP, Cancer (cervical, breast, oral), Hypo-thyroid, Obesity,
Diabetes

Q3. Advantages and Disadvantages of Screening  ⭐⭐⭐⭐ (May 2024 — NEW)
Advantages of Screening
Advantage
Explanation / Example
Early detection
Disease caught at preclinical stage → better
prognosis

Key Biases in Screening — Must Know
Lead time bias: Survival appears longer because clock starts earlier (at screening), not because
patient lives longer
Length bias: Screening preferentially detects slow-growing tumours; aggressive tumours grow too
fast to be caught between screens
Volunteer bias (Selection bias): Screened population is healthier than non-screened — inflates
apparent benefit
Overdiagnosis bias: Detecting pseudodisease — abnormality that would never cause symptoms
or death
- High Yield: Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT) is the GOLD STANDARD to evaluate screening
programmes — eliminates lead time and length biases.
- Mnemonic: Biases: "LLV-O" — Lead time, Length, Volunteer, Overdiagnosis

Q4. Concept of Screening — Types, Criteria and Effectiveness  ⭐⭐⭐ (2018)
119

Concept of Screening
Screening is a secondary prevention strategy — does not prevent disease but detects it early to
improve outcomes. It is applied at the interface between primary prevention (preventing disease onset)
and tertiary prevention (limiting disability).
The concept rests on the idea that most chronic diseases have a preclinical detectable phase (PCDP)
during which intervention is more effective than after clinical disease onset.
Types of Screening (Detailed Classification)
By population covered: Mass → High-risk → Individual (case finding)
By setting: Community-based, hospital-based, workplace-based, school-based
By number of tests: Single test vs Multiphasic (battery of tests applied simultaneously)
By timing: Antenatal, neonatal, premarital, occupational, geriatric
By disease type: Communicable (TB — CBNAAT, sputum), NCD (HTN, DM, cancer), genetic
(sickle cell)
Criteria for Effectiveness of Screening
A screening programme is considered effective when:
↓ Specific mortality: Death rate from the target disease falls (RCT evidence required)
↓ Incidence of advanced disease: More cases detected at early stage
↑ Survival: True survival gain, not just lead time bias
Cost-effective: Cost per QALY/DALY averted within acceptable threshold
Acceptable false positive rate: <5–10% to avoid excessive follow-up burden
Evaluation Designs for Screening
RCT: Gold standard — randomize to screened vs unscreened; compare cause-specific mortality
Case-control study: Compare past screening history among cases vs controls
Cohort study: Follow screened vs unscreened — compare outcomes
Note: Survival rate is a POOR endpoint due to lead time and length biases — use disease-specific

MORTALITY RATE

Examples of Evaluated Screening Programmes
Cervical cancer (Pap/VIA): Proven to ↓ mortality — adopted in >100 countries
Breast cancer (mammography): ↓ mortality by ~15–20% in RCTs for women 50–69 yrs
Colorectal cancer (FOBT): ↓ mortality by ~15–33% in RCTs
Neonatal PKU: Dietary treatment from birth prevents intellectual disability
Prostate cancer (PSA): CONTROVERSIAL — high false positives, overdiagnosis; not
recommended for mass screening
- High Yield: Wilson & Jungner 10 criteria are the standard for deciding whether to implement a
screening programme. Memorize all 10.
- Mnemonic: Effectiveness = ↓ Mortality (not ↑ Survival) — True benefit shown only in RCT

Q1. Discuss demographic cycle with suitable examples. ★★★★★ (2022, 2012)

Introduction
The Demographic Cycle (also called Demographic Transition Theory) describes the sequential change
in birth rates and death rates in a society as it progresses from a pre-industrial to an industrialized
economy. It was proposed by Warren Thompson (1929) and elaborated by Frank Notestein (1945).

Stages of the Demographic Cycle
The theory identifies FIVE stages (some sources describe 4), each characterized by distinct patterns of
birth rate (BR), death rate (DR), and natural growth rate (NGR = BR - DR):

Stage
Birth Rate
Death Rate
Growth Rate

(20-30/1000)

Detailed Description of Each Stage
Stage I – High Stationary (Pre-Industrial): Both birth rate and death rate are high (40–50/1000).
Population remains stable/stagnant. High mortality due to famines, epidemics, and wars. No family
planning. Example: Pre-colonial India, Medieval Europe.
Stage II – Early Expanding (Industrial Revolution Phase): Death rate falls due to improved
sanitation, nutrition, and medical advances. Birth rate remains high. Population EXPLODES. This is
the most dangerous phase for resource management. Example: India in 1920s–1970s.
Stage III – Late Expanding (Transitional): Birth rate begins to fall due to urbanization, education
of women, use of contraception, rise in cost of living. Death rate is already low and stable. Growth
decelerates. Example: India currently (BR ~20, DR ~7/1000).
Stage IV – Low Stationary (Post-Industrial): Both birth and death rates are low. Zero population
growth. Aging population. Example: Western Europe, North America.
Stage V – Declining: Birth rate falls BELOW death rate → negative natural growth rate →
population shrinks. Concerns: labour shortage, pension burden. Example: Germany (BR 9, DR
12/1000), Japan.
122

India's Position in Demographic Cycle
India is currently in STAGE III (Late Expanding) of the Demographic Transition:
Birth Rate: ~20/1000 (2020 SRS data)
Death Rate: ~6.2/1000
Natural Growth Rate: ~13.8/1000
TFR: 2.0 (National Family Health Survey-5, 2019-21) — near replacement level
15 states have already achieved TFR ≤ 2.1 (replacement level)

Demographic Gap
The gap between the falling death rate and the (slower-falling) birth rate is called the 'Demographic Gap.'
This gap = period of maximum population growth. India's demographic gap was widest in 1960s–1980s.

- The Demographic Transition Theory explains population dynamics through 5 stages. India
is in Stage III with a TFR of 2.0, approaching replacement level. Key determinants: education,
urbanization, female literacy, family planning programs.
- Exam Tip: Draw the classic S-curve/J-curve diagram showing BR and DR trends across stages.
Examiners reward visual representation.

123

Q2. What are the different processes of demography? Discuss fertility indicators.

- (2023)

Processes of Demography
Demography is the scientific study of human populations. The three fundamental demographic processes
are:

1. Fertility: The reproductive performance of a population — refers to actual live births occurring in
a population.
2. Mortality: Deaths occurring in a population. Measured by death rate, IMR, MMR, etc.
3. Migration: Movement of people into (immigration) or out of (emigration) a geographic area.
Internal migration (rural-to-urban) is a major driver of demographic change in India.

These three processes determine:
Population size
Population composition (age, sex distribution)
Population distribution (urban vs rural)

Fertility Indicators — Detailed Classification

A. Crude Birth Rate (CBR)
Definition: Number of live births per 1000 mid-year population in a given year.
Formula: CBR = (Number of live births / Mid-year total population) × 1000
Normal range: High: >40; Moderate: 20–40; Low: <20
India (2020): ~20/1000
Limitation: Includes entire population in denominator (including non-women of reproductive age)
— hence crude.

B. General Fertility Rate (GFR)
Formula: GFR = (Live births / Women aged 15–49 years) × 1000
More refined than CBR as denominator is restricted to women of reproductive age (WRA).

C. Age-Specific Fertility Rate (ASFR)
Formula: ASFR = (Live births to women in age group X / Mid-year women in age group X) × 1000
Calculated for each 5-year age group: 15–19, 20–24, 25–29, 30–34, 35–39, 40–44, 45–49.
Use: Identifies peak fertile age; basis for calculating TFR

D. Total Fertility Rate (TFR) — MOST IMPORTANT
Definition: Average number of children a woman would have if she lived through her entire reproductive
period (15–49 years) at currently observed ASFR.
124

Formula: TFR = 5 × Σ(ASFR for all 7 age groups) — multiplied by 5 as each age group spans 5 years.
TFR Value
Interpretation
2.1
Replacement Level Fertility (population stable over generations)

>2.1

Growing population

<2.1

Declining population (long-term)
India TFR (NFHS-5, 2019-21)
2.0 — Below replacement level for the first time!
Bihar (highest)
3.0
Sikkim (lowest)
1.1

E. Gross Reproduction Rate (GRR)
Average number of DAUGHTERS a woman would bear during her lifetime, assuming no mortality.
Formula: GRR = TFR × (proportion of female births) ≈ TFR × 0.488
Replacement value: GRR = 1.0

F. Net Reproduction Rate (NRR)
Average number of daughters that would replace each woman in the next generation, after accounting
for mortality.
Formula: NRR = GRR × (probability of surviving to mean age of childbearing)
Interpretation: NRR = 1: replacement; NRR > 1: growing; NRR < 1: declining population
India: NRR ≈ 0.9 (NFHS-5)

- TFR is the single most important fertility indicator. India's TFR = 2.0 (NFHS-5) — below
replacement level of 2.1 for the first time. NRR and GRR account for sex composition and
mortality respectively.

125

Q3. Significance of Demographic Cycle and Demographic Gap. ★★★★ (May 2024)

★NEW★

Significance of the Demographic Cycle
The demographic cycle has wide-ranging implications for public health policy, economic planning, and
resource allocation:

1. Policy Planning: Governments use the stage of demographic transition to design policies (family
planning, old-age security, healthcare infrastructure).
2. Health System Planning: Stage I/II: Maternal & child health infrastructure needed. Stage IV/V:
Geriatric care services required.
3. Economic Implications: Population growth in Stage II/III strains resources. Aging population in
Stage IV creates dependency burden.
4. Predicting Population Trends: Helps forecast future population size, age structure, and
dependency ratios.
5. Family Planning Programs: Understanding the stage guides contraceptive acceptance
campaigns and reproductive health priorities.

Significance of Demographic Gap
The Demographic Gap is defined as the period/interval between the decline in death rate and the
(lagging) decline in birth rate. It represents the phase of MAXIMUM population explosion.

Key significance:
Explains why population explodes even without increasing birth rates — it is the FALLING DEATH
RATE that drives it.
India's demographic gap: Largest in 1960s–1980s; currently narrowing as birth rate falls.
A wide demographic gap = high NGR = more pressure on food, education, employment, healthcare.
Guides urgency of family planning interventions to accelerate birth rate decline.
Countries with a narrowing demographic gap have potential for a 'Demographic Dividend'.

- The demographic cycle informs health and economic policy at every stage. The
demographic gap explains population momentum and the urgent need for fertility control
measures in developing nations.

126

Population Transition — Definition
Population Transition refers to the shift from a regime of high fertility and high mortality to one of low
fertility and low mortality. It encompasses the Demographic Transition Theory but focuses specifically on
health consequences.

Q7. Describe Significance of Demographic Dividend in India's Development. ★★★

(2023)

Definition of Demographic Dividend
Demographic Dividend is the accelerated economic growth potential resulting from a shift in a
population's age structure — specifically, when the proportion of working-age population (15–64 years)
is LARGER than the non-working age population (children + elderly). This creates a 'window of
opportunity' for rapid economic growth.

India's Demographic Window of Opportunity
India's working-age population (15–64 yrs): ~65% of total population
Median age: 28.4 years (one of the world's youngest)
Dependency ratio: ~48 per 100 working-age persons (improving)
Window of Opportunity: Estimated 2005–2055 — India has 3 DECADES remaining

Significance for India's Development

1. Economic Growth: More workers → higher production → higher GDP. East Asian 'Tiger'
economies gained 25–40% of their GDP growth from demographic dividend (1960s–1990s).
2. Higher Savings Rate: Working-age adults save more than children/elderly → capital
accumulation → investment.
3. Human Capital Development: Smaller families = more investment per child in education and
health → higher productivity.
4. Women's Empowerment: Lower fertility → more women in workforce → double income
households.
5. Innovation and Entrepreneurship: Young workforce drives technological innovation (IT,
startups — India's strength).
6. Better Health Outcomes: Working-age adults generally healthier → reduced healthcare burden.

Conditions for Realizing Demographic Dividend
Quality education and skill development (NEP 2020, Skill India Mission)
Adequate job creation (need 8-10 million jobs/year)
Healthcare investment to keep workforce healthy
Women's participation in workforce
Good governance and rule of law

Challenges
Unemployment and underemployment — youth bulge without jobs = liability not asset
Skill mismatch — education not aligned with market needs
Regional disparity — North India still has young population, South aging
COVID-19 setback on education and employment

130

UNIT 16: PRIMARY HEALTH CARE / COMMUNITY

PARTICIPATION

Q1. Define PHC and Discuss Community Participation in Health and Its Advantages.

- (2020)

Definition of Primary Health Care (PHC)
As per the Alma-Ata Declaration (1978): 'Primary Health Care is essential health care based on practical,
scientifically sound and socially acceptable methods and technology made universally accessible to
individuals and families in the community through their full participation and at a cost that the community
and country can afford to maintain at every stage of their development in the spirit of self-reliance and
self-determination.'

Key Elements of PHC (8 Pillars — SAFE CARE)
Element
Description
Safe water & sanitation
Clean drinking water, basic sanitation infrastructure
Adequate nutrition
Food security and nutritional supplementation
Family welfare
Maternal & child health, family planning services
Education about health
Health education and health promotion
Control of endemic diseases
Malaria, TB, leprosy, etc.
Appropriate treatment
Treatment of common diseases and injuries
Referral services
Link to secondary and tertiary care
Expanded Programme on
Immunization
Immunization of children and mothers

Community Participation in Health — Definition
Community participation refers to the process by which individuals and communities take an active role
in identifying health problems, planning and implementing solutions, and evaluating their effectiveness.
It is a CORNERSTONE of PHC philosophy.

Levels of Community Participation (Arnstein's Ladder)
Level 1 — Manipulation: Community is manipulated to accept top-down decisions. Lowest form —
NOT true participation.
Level 2 — Information/Consultation: Community is informed or consulted but decisions remain
with authorities.
Level 3 — Placation: Community advice is considered but may not be acted upon.
Level 4 — Partnership: Community co-manages programs — shared decision-making.
Level 5 — Delegated Power: Community controls major aspects of the health program.
131

Level 6 — Community Control: Community has full autonomous control. Highest form — TRUE
empowerment.

Advantages of Community Participation in Health

1. Relevance and Appropriateness: Health programs designed with community input address
actual felt needs rather than perceived needs of planners.
2. Increased Utilization: Services designed with community involvement have better uptake —
people use what they helped create.
3. Sustainability: Community ownership ensures programs continue even after external support
ends.
4. Cost-effectiveness: Community resources (labor, local knowledge, volunteers like ASHA)
reduce program costs dramatically.
5. Social Mobilization: Collective action addresses social determinants of health (water, sanitation,
nutrition) more effectively.
6. Empowerment: Communities develop capacity to identify and solve problems independently.
7. Health Equity: Marginalized communities can advocate for their specific health needs.
8. Monitoring and Accountability: Community members can monitor program quality and hold
providers accountable.

Examples in India
ASHA (Accredited Social Health Activist) — community health worker embedded in villages; trained
local woman; 1 per 1000 population
Village Health Sanitation & Nutrition Committees (VHSNCs) — community governance of health
Jan Arogya Samitis — hospital-level community committees
Mahila Arogya Samitis — women's health groups under NHM

- PHC cannot succeed without community participation. The Alma-Ata Declaration's vision of
'Health for All by 2000' and the Astana Declaration (2018) renewal both emphasize community
participation as non-negotiable. India's ASHA program is the world's largest community
health worker program.

132

Q2. Role of Community in PHC. Differentiate Community Involvement vs Community
Participation. ★★★★ (2021)

Role of Community in PHC

1. Needs Assessment: Community members identify health priorities relevant to their local context
(not just epidemiological data).
2. Planning: Contribute local knowledge, cultural perspectives, and resource assessment to health
program design.
3. Resource Mobilization: Contribute labor, land, money, and materials; reduce dependency on
government funding.
4. Implementation: ASHAs, traditional birth attendants (TBAs), village health committees
implement programs at ground level.
5. Monitoring & Evaluation: Community monitors quality of services received; provides feedback;
holds system accountable.
6. Advocacy: Communities advocate for their rights to health services and voice grievances.
7. Social Norms Change: Community leaders and groups promote health-seeking behavior,
immunization acceptance, hygiene practices.

Community Involvement vs Community Participation — Differentiation

designed by external agencies

evaluation

authorities; community executes

- Community involvement = community as MEANS to achieve externally set goals.
Community participation = community as END in itself — empowerment and self-reliance.
PHC demands participation, not merely involvement.
133

Q3. How Can Communities Be More Involved in Making Decisions About Their Own
Health and Wellbeing? ★★★★ (Dec 2024) ★NEW★

Strategies for Greater Community Decision-Making in Health

1. Strengthening Community Health Institutions
Village Health, Sanitation and Nutrition Committees (VHSNCs) — decentralize health decisions to
gram panchayat level
Rogi Kalyan Samitis (Patient Welfare Committees) at health facilities — community oversight of
hospital governance
Mahila Arogya Samitis — women-led groups for reproductive and child health decisions

2. Community Needs Assessment and Participatory Research
Participatory Rural Appraisal (PRA) tools: Focus Group Discussions (FGDs), social mapping,
seasonal calendars
Community diagnosis involving local people in data collection and priority-setting
Community scorecards — self-assessment tools for monitoring health program quality

3. Training and Capacity Building
ASHA training modules include community mobilization and advocacy skills
Panchayati Raj members trained in health governance
Digital health literacy — enable communities to access health information

4. Legal and Policy Mechanisms
Right to Information (RTI) Act — communities can demand health data
Jan Sunwai (Public Hearings) — community forums to voice health grievances
Social audit of health schemes (MGNREGA model applied to health)

5. Intersectoral Action
Health decisions cannot be separated from water, sanitation, education, livelihood
Multi-sectoral convergence at village level: ASHA + Anganwadi worker + school teacher + ANM
Swachh Bharat Mission — community decision on toilet construction

6. Addressing Barriers to Participation
Include marginalized groups: women, SC/ST, elderly, migrants
Conduct meetings at times/places accessible to all community members
Use local language — avoid medical jargon
Address power dynamics — prevent elite capture of community committees

7. Technology-Enabled Community Engagement
134

mHealth apps in local languages for health information and feedback
Aarogya Setu, CoWIN — digital community health platforms
Community radio stations for health promotion

- The Declaration of Astana (2018) reaffirms that communities must be empowered — not
just consulted — in health decisions. Key mechanisms: VHSNCs, social audits, PRAs, and
RTI. Equity requires special focus on women, tribals, and the poor.

135

Q4. Critical Review of Role and Responsibilities of [Health Workers in PHC]

Key Health Workers in PHC — Roles and Responsibilities

1. ASHA (Accredited Social Health Activist)
Selection: Literate woman from the village (preferably 10th pass); 1 per 1000 rural population;
selected by gram sabha
Training: 23 modules; trained by ANM/LHV; performance-based incentives

Key Responsibilities:
Community mobilization for immunization, ANC, institutional delivery (JSY)
Home visits — identify pregnant women, sick children, TB suspects
Facilitate access to health services — escort and link community to PHC
Health education on ORS, nutrition, WASH, contraception
Drug distribution: ORS packets, iron tablets, contraceptives, chlorine tablets
CBAC form filling for NCD screening

Critical Review of ASHA:
STRENGTHS: World's largest CHW program (~1 million ASHAs); proven impact on IMR, MMR,
immunization
CHALLENGES: Overloaded with multiple programs; inadequate and irregular incentives; lack of
career growth; burnout

2. ANM (Auxiliary Nurse Midwife)
Position: Works at Sub-Centre (SC) level; 1 SC per 3000-5000 rural population
Key Responsibilities:
Conduct deliveries and antenatal/postnatal care
Immunization — cold chain maintenance, outreach sessions
Family planning counselling and method provision
Malaria slide collection, TB sputum collection
Maintain vital statistics (births, deaths)
Supervise and support ASHA workers

3. Medical Officer (PHC)
Position: 1 MO per PHC (population 20,000-30,000 plains; 20,000 tribal/hilly)
Key Responsibilities:
Outpatient consultation — diagnosis and treatment of common diseases
Minor surgical procedures — suturing, abscess drainage
Supervision of all health staff at PHC and sub-centres
Referral decisions to CHC/District Hospital
National health program implementation and reporting
136

Community health education and awareness activities

4. Health Inspector / Sanitary Inspector
Environmental sanitation — safe water, solid waste management, vector control
Food safety inspections
Epidemic investigation and reporting

subsections.title: 17. DESCRIPTIVE EPIDEMIOLOGY / TIME TRENDS
subsections.content:
Q1. Define epidemiology. Describe procedures involved in conducting descriptive
studies.
  - Year: 2021, 2018   |   Frequency: High Priority

INTRODUCTION

Epidemiology is the cornerstone of public health and preventive medicine. It provides a scientific framework
for understanding the distribution of diseases and the factors that influence them. The knowledge generated
by epidemiology guides the planning and evaluation of public health interventions and health care services.

DEFINITION OF EPIDEMIOLOGY

The most widely accepted definition is that of John M. Last (2001): "Epidemiology is the study of the
distribution and determinants of health-related states or events in specified populations, and the application
of this study to the control of health problems."
Distribution: refers to the frequency and pattern of health events in terms of Person, Place, and Time.
Determinants: refers to the causal factors — biological, physical, social, or environmental — that influence
the occurrence of disease.
Specified populations: emphasizes the population-based (not individual) nature of epidemiology.
Application: epidemiology is always goal-directed — to prevent and control disease.

SCOPE & AIMS OF EPIDEMIOLOGY

- To describe the distribution of disease in terms of Person, Place, and Time
- To identify the determinants and risk factors of disease
139

COMMUNITY MEDICINE — GOLD MEDAL LONG ANSWERS  |  Topics 17 & 18: Descriptive Epidemiology & Determinants of
Health
For Exam Use Only  |  Prepared with Gold Standard Content
- To study the natural history and prognosis of disease
- To evaluate preventive and therapeutic measures
- To aid in health planning and policy formulation

TYPES OF EPIDEMIOLOGIC STUDIES

Type
Sub-type
Key Feature
Descriptive
Cross-sectional, Case report, Case
series
Describes disease distribution (Who,
Where, When)
Analytical
Case-control, Cohort
Tests hypotheses about causation
Experimental
RCT, Field Trial, Community Trial
Investigates effects of interventions

PROCEDURES IN CONDUCTING A DESCRIPTIVE STUDY

A descriptive study systematically collects and analyzes data to describe the occurrence of a disease or
health event in a population. The following steps are followed:
Step 1: Define the Problem
The health problem to be studied is clearly defined. This includes specifying the disease or health event,
identifying the affected population, and the time period of interest. Operational definitions of the case must
be established (e.g., clinical, laboratory, or epidemiological definitions).
Step 2: Review of Existing Literature
A comprehensive review of published literature, surveillance data, health records, vital statistics, and
hospital registers is done to understand what is already known about the disease. This helps identify gaps
in knowledge and formulate research questions.
Step 3: Define the Study Population
The target population is clearly identified. Inclusion and exclusion criteria are established. Sampling
methods (random, systematic, stratified) are applied to select representative subjects to ensure
generalizability.
Step 4: Data Collection
Data are gathered using primary or secondary methods. Primary data are collected through questionnaires,
interviews, physical examinations, and biological specimens. Secondary data come from hospital records,
disease registries, census data, and mortality statistics. Variables collected include:
- Person variables: Age, sex, race, ethnicity, socioeconomic status, occupation, marital status,
religion, lifestyle.
- Place variables: Country, region, urban/rural classification, geographic clusters, work environment.
- Time variables: Onset of illness, seasonal patterns, epidemic curves, secular trends.
Step 5: Data Organization & Analysis
Collected data are tabulated, classified, and analyzed using appropriate statistical methods. Rates and
ratios (incidence rate, prevalence rate, attack rate, mortality rate, case fatality rate) are calculated. Graphs,
maps, spot maps, and epidemic curves are constructed to visualize patterns.
Step 6: Interpretation & Hypothesis Generation
Patterns identified are interpreted in terms of Person, Place, and Time. From the observed patterns,
hypotheses about the causative factors are generated. Descriptive studies do NOT test hypotheses — they
only generate them for further analytical investigation.
Step 7: Report and Communicate Findings
140

COMMUNITY MEDICINE — GOLD MEDAL LONG ANSWERS  |  Topics 17 & 18: Descriptive Epidemiology & Determinants of
Health
For Exam Use Only  |  Prepared with Gold Standard Content
Findings are documented and communicated to relevant stakeholders — public health authorities, the
scientific community, and policy makers. Recommendations for preventive and control measures are made
based on the findings.
EXAM TIP: Remember: Descriptive studies answer WHO, WHERE, and WHEN. Analytical studies answer
WHY and HOW. RCTs answer whether an intervention WORKS. This hierarchy is frequently tested.
CONCLUSION: Descriptive epidemiology is the foundation of public health practice. By systematically
collecting, organizing, and analyzing data on Person, Place, and Time, it generates hypotheses that
analytical studies then test, ultimately leading to disease prevention and health promotion.

  Q2. Classify epidemiologic studies. Describe steps in conducting a cohort study.
  - Year: 2023   |   Frequency: High Priority

INTRODUCTION

Epidemiologic studies are systematic investigations designed to describe or analyze the distribution and
determinants of diseases in human populations. They are broadly classified into Observational Studies and
Experimental Studies.

CLASSIFICATION OF EPIDEMIOLOGIC STUDIES

I. Observational Studies
The investigator does not intervene; they merely observe events as they occur naturally.
A. Descriptive Studies: Describe the frequency and distribution of disease without testing any specific
hypothesis.
- Case Report: Detailed clinical description of a single patient with an unusual disease.
- Case Series: Description of a small group of patients with the same condition.
- Cross-Sectional (Prevalence) Study: Measures disease and exposure simultaneously at one point
in time. Gives Prevalence.
B. Analytical Studies: Test specific hypotheses about the relationship between exposure and disease.
- Case-Control Study: Begins with disease (cases) vs. no disease (controls); looks backward at
exposure. Gives Odds Ratio (OR). Best for RARE diseases.
- Cohort Study: Begins with exposure; follows forward for disease outcome. Gives Relative Risk
(RR). Best for RARE exposures.
II. Experimental Studies
The investigator actively intervenes and controls the exposure or treatment.
- Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT): Gold standard. Participants randomly assigned to treatment or
control group.
- Field Trial: Conducted in the community among healthy people at risk (e.g., vaccine trials).
- Community Trial: Entire communities assigned to interventions (e.g., fluoridation of water).
Feature
Case-Control
Cohort
RCT
Direction
Backward
(Retrospective)
Forward (Prospective)
Forward

Disease status
Exposure status
Random assignment
Measure
Odds Ratio (OR)
Relative Risk (RR)
Relative Risk / NNT

Rare diseases
Rare exposures
Testing interventions
141

COMMUNITY MEDICINE — GOLD MEDAL LONG ANSWERS  |  Topics 17 & 18: Descriptive Epidemiology & Determinants of
Health
For Exam Use Only  |  Prepared with Gold Standard Content
Feature
Case-Control
Cohort
RCT
Time
